Select 3–4 wide-angled scaffolds at 24–36" up. | | Year 2 (Jan–Feb) | Continue leader to next tier at 24–30" higher. Cut last year's leader to 1/2 above bud. | | Year 3 (Jan–Feb) | Develop next tier. Begin selecting permanent fruiting wood. Remove vertical water sprouts. | | Year 4+ (Jan–Feb) | Maintenance: remove water sprouts, dead wood, crossing branches. Maintain spurs on spur-bearing varieties. Tip back the leader to keep height manageable. |

sh: Pome fruits — apples and pears — 2 Pears — Asian outperforms European in the Dragoons
b: European pears (Pyrus communis — Bartlett, Comice, Anjou) are workable in the Dragoons but are stressed by the alkaline soil, the heat, and fireblight — a bacterial disease that the European pears are highly susceptible to. Asian pears (Pyrus pyrifolia — Shinseiki, Hosui, Kosui) are better adapted: more alkaline-tolerant, more heat-tolerant, much more fireblight-resistant. For the Dragoons, start with Asian pears.

sh: Pome fruits — apples and pears — 2 Pears — Asian outperforms European in the Dragoons — Variety reference — pears
b: | Variety | Type | Chill h | Harvest | Fireblight resistance | Dragoons fit | | Shinseiki | Asian (P. pyrifolia) | 400 | Aug | Excellent | ●●●●● — Best Dragoon pear. Crisp, sweet, productive. | | Hosui | Asian | 450 | Aug–Sep | Excellent | ●●●●● — Russet skin; very sweet. | | Kosui | Asian | 450 | Aug | Excellent | ●●●●○ — Sweet, early. | | 20th Century (Nijisseiki) | Asian | 450 | Aug–Sep | Good | ●●●●○ — Classic Japanese variety. | | Bartlett | European (P. communis) | 600 | Aug | Poor | ●●●●○ — Workable; watch for fireblight | | Comice | European | 600 | Sep–Oct | Moderate | ●●●●○ — Buttery dessert pear | | Anjou | European | 800 | Sep | Moderate | ●●●○○ — Workable at higher Dragoon elevations | | Kieffer | European × Asian hybrid | 350 | Oct | Excellent | ●●●●○ — Tough, prolific, cooking pear |

sh: Pome fruits — apples and pears — 2 Pears — Asian outperforms European in the Dragoons — Pollination — pears require cross-pollination
b: Like apples, almost all pears require cross-pollination. Asian pears cross-pollinate with European pears, so a mixed planting works. The classic backyard combinations: | Combination | Reason | | Shinseiki + Hosui ● | Both Asian; both fireblight-resistant; bloom overlap reliable | | Bartlett + Comice | Both European; classic dessert combo | | Shinseiki + Bartlett | Asian + European cross-pollination — fine |

sh: Pome fruits — apples and pears — 2 Pears — Asian outperforms European in the Dragoons — Fireblight — the major pear disease
b: Erwinia amylovora infects pear flowers in spring during warm humid weather, then progresses down the branch killing the wood. Symptoms are a blackened "shepherd's crook" at the tip of a branch — the wilted tip looks like it's been scorched by fire. Once established, the only control is aggressive pruning of infected wood 12" below the visible damage, with pruner sterilization between every cut. Pruning out fireblight early is the difference between losing a branch and losing the tree. For the Dragoons, fireblight is moderate in pressure — drier than Pacific Northwest pear country but a real concern in monsoon years. Plant fireblight-resistant varieties (Asian pears, Kieffer); avoid heavy nitrogen fertilization (lush growth invites infection); prune aggressively at the first sign.

sh: Pome fruits — apples and pears — 3 Codling moth — the apple pest
b: Codling moth (Cydia pomonella) is the dominant pest of apples in the Dragoons and most of the temperate world. The adult moth lays eggs on developing fruit in late spring; larvae bore into the apple and feed at the core; the result is the classic "wormy apple." A single apple tree can lose 60–80 % of fruit to codling moth in an unmanaged orchard. Control is integrated: Pheromone traps for monitoring — hang in trees in April; check weekly. The trap catches the first flight and tells you when to act. Bagging fruit — at fruit thinning (4–6 weeks after bloom), slide a paper or nylon footie bag over each developing apple. Tedious but very effective for backyard scale. Mating disruption — pheromone twist-ties distributed through the orchard confuse males and prevent mating. Effective at scale (1 acre+); not really practical for a 2-tree backyard. Spinosad spray at first egg-hatch (use trap counts to time). Repeat in 10–14 days. Trunk banding — wrap trunks with corrugated cardboard in June; larvae crawl into the cardboard to pupate; remove and burn in fall. Sanitation — pick up fallen apples promptly; don't leave wormy fruit on the ground over winter (larvae overwinter in soil). Codling moth granulosis virus (CYD-X) — biological control, applied as a foliar spray; targets only codling moth larvae. For a 2–4 tree backyard, the bagging + sanitation combo works well and uses no sprays. For larger plantings, monitoring traps + spinosad at peak egg-hatch is the standard.

sh: Citrus — container-only
b: Why in-ground doesn't work · Meyer lemon, Persian lime, kaffir lime · soil mix · wintering Cochise County is too cold for in-ground citrus. The hardy citrus species (Meyer lemon, Persian lime, kumquat, kaffir lime, calamondin) survive brief frost down to about 28 °F but are damaged below 25 °F and killed below 20 °F. The Dragoons see 3–6 nights per winter below 25 °F and occasional January lows below 20 °F. An in-ground citrus tree, no matter how well-sited, will die in 1 to 5 winters. Don't plant citrus in the ground in the Dragoons. Don't plant in-ground citrus. Tucson and Phoenix grow citrus reliably in-ground at 2,000–2,500 ft. Cochise County at 4,500–5,000 ft is too cold. The investment in a citrus tree that dies its second winter is one of the most predictable failures in this guide. The workable approach for citrus in the Dragoons is container-only, with the trees brought indoors during cold weather and set back outside in warm weather. Done well, container citrus produces good crops of Meyer lemon, Persian lime, and kaffir lime year-round.

sh: Citrus — container-only — 1 What to grow in containers
b: | Variety | Latin | Cold tolerance | Container size | Indoor light? | Notes | | Meyer lemon | Citrus × meyeri | 28 °F (brief) | 15–25 gal | Tolerates indoors | The classic Dragoons container citrus. Productive. | | Persian lime (Bearss) | Citrus × latifolia | 28 °F | 15–25 gal | Tolerates indoors | Productive; tart; ships well | | Kaffir lime | Citrus hystrix | 30 °F | 10–15 gal | Tolerates indoors | Grown for leaves more than fruit | | Kumquat (Meiwa, Nagami) | Fortunella spp. | 25 °F | 10–15 gal | Tolerates indoors | Cold-hardiest citrus; small fruit eaten whole | | Calamondin | Citrus × microcarpa | 28 °F | 10–15 gal | Tolerates indoors | Ornamental + sour-orange substitute | | Satsuma mandarin | Citrus reticulata | 25 °F | 15–25 gal | Tolerates indoors | Sweet, easy-peel; the hardiest sweet citrus | | Mexican lime | Citrus aurantifolia | 32 °F | 10–15 gal | Yes | True key lime — fussy; needs warm winter shelter | Avoid sweet oranges (Valencia, Navel) and grapefruit — they require more heat than container life provides at our latitude.

sh: Citrus — container-only — 2 Soil mix for citrus pots
b: Citrus dislike water-logged feet and benefit from a fast-draining, slightly acidic mix. The standard recipe: 50 % high-quality bagged potting mix (Pro-Mix BX or equivalent) 25 % coarse perlite (improves drainage) 25 % composted pine bark fines (acidifies, improves drainage) Top-dress with 1/2" gravel mulch to retain moisture and prevent soil splashing Citrus do best at pH 6.0–6.5, which is below Cochise well-water pH. Treat irrigation water for citrus pots the same way you would treat hydroponic reservoir water — drop the pH to ~6.2 with citric acid or phosphoric acid (see Guide 4 Part 3 for the canonical pH-down recipe). Without water treatment, container citrus develop interveinal chlorosis from iron lockout within a year.

sh: Citrus — container-only — 3 Wintering indoors
b: The standard rhythm: March–November: outside, in full sun on a south-facing patio. Water 2–3× per week in summer. First frost forecast below 32 °F (typically October 25–November 15): bring indoors. Place in a south-facing window, sunroom, or under grow lights. November–February: indoor light + reduced watering. Citrus go semi-dormant in low light; over-watering at this stage causes root rot. March, after last frost forecast (typically March 25–April 5): transition outdoors over 7–10 days, starting in part shade and gradually exposing to full sun. Citrus flower indoors in winter (Meyer lemons reliably bloom around January–February). Hand-pollinate with a small paintbrush moved between flowers to set fruit indoors. The south-facing patio rule. A south-facing wall or patio is the right outdoor home for container citrus from March through November. Radiant heat from the wall extends the frost-free window by 2–3 weeks on each end, and the wall blocks the cold north wind. Place pots on casters so they roll in and out easily — saves a great deal of back strain over the years.

sh: Citrus — container-only — 4 Fertilizing container citrus
b: Citrus are heavy feeders, particularly for nitrogen and micronutrients. Use a balanced citrus fertilizer (often labeled "Citrus & Avocado") at half-strength monthly from March through October. The DIY hydroponic Masterblend recipe (see Guide 4 Part 1) works fine as a liquid feed for citrus pots at standard fruiting-stage EC (1.6–2.0 mS/cm) — citrus are happy on the same nutrient profile as fruiting tomatoes and peppers. Foliar iron and zinc sprays (see Guide 4 Part 2) are useful 2–3 times per growing season for citrus in pots; the alkaline tap water and tightly-confined root zone lock out micronutrients faster than in-ground trees.

sh: Irrigation for the orchard
b: Drip + deep soak · first-year establishment vs mature trees · gallons per tree per week Orchard irrigation is different from annual-crop irrigation in two important ways. First, the root zone is deeper — tree roots can extend 6–10 ft down on a Dragoons site with broken caliche, so the irrigation schedule should deliver water deep enough to wet that whole profile, not just the top 8". Second, the schedule shifts seasonally with a much bigger range than vegetables — a mature peach in February uses essentially zero gallons; the same tree in August uses 50+ gallons per week. The irrigation system must accommodate both extremes.

sh: Irrigation for the orchard — 1 Drip irrigation layout
b: The standard backyard orchard irrigation setup: Mainline — 1" poly tubing from the well or storage tank, on a slope down to the trees Sub-mains — 1/2" poly tubing branching off the main, looping to each tree Emitters per tree — 4–6 emitters at year 1; expanding to 8–12 emitters for mature trees, arranged in a ring at the dripline (the projection of the canopy's outermost edge) Emitter flow rate — 1 gph (or 2 gph for high-summer demand) Timer — RAINPOINT or Rachio battery-operated timer with multiple stations Filter — 200-mesh inline filter at the mainline (Dragoon well water carries enough sediment to clog emitters within a season) The emitter ring expands outward as the tree grows. A young tree has emitters within 12" of the trunk; a mature tree has emitters in a ring 4–6 ft out from the trunk at the dripline. Don't water the trunk — water the root zone, which is where the feeder roots live.

sh: Irrigation for the orchard — 2 First-year establishment vs mature trees
b: A newly-planted tree has all its roots in the original rootball (or, for bareroot, in a small clump of new roots). It cannot draw water from a wide area. It needs frequent, light watering — every 2–3 days in summer, focused close to the trunk. As the roots expand, the watering shifts to deeper and less frequent. | Year | Watering frequency (summer) | Watering depth | Emitter pattern | | 1 (newly planted) | Every 2–3 days | Wet the original rootball + 12" beyond | 4 emitters within 18" of trunk | | 2 | Every 3–4 days | Wet to 24" depth | 6 emitters at 24" radius | | 3 | Every 5–7 days | Wet to 36" depth | 8 emitters at 36" radius | | Mature (5+ yr) | Every 7–10 days | Wet to 60" depth | 10–12 emitters at dripline (4–6 ft) | The principle: as the tree matures, water less often but more deeply. Deep watering encourages deep rooting, which makes the tree more drought-tolerant. Frequent shallow watering keeps the roots in the top foot of soil, where they dry out fast and where the tree is fragile during heat waves.

sh: Irrigation for the orchard — 3 Gallons per tree per week by season
b: | Season | Months | Fig | Pomegranate | Jujube | Peach | Apricot | Plum | Apple | Pear | | Dormant | Dec–Feb | 0–4 | 0–4 | 0–4 | 0–6 | 0–6 | 0–6 | 0–6 | 0–6 | | Bud break / bloom | Mar–Apr | 4–8 | 4–6 | 4–6 | 8–12 | 8–12 | 8–12 | 8–12 | 8–12 | | Fruit set / sizing | May–Jun | 15–25 | 10–15 | 10–15 | 20–30 | 20–30 | 20–30 | 15–25 | 15–25 | | Hot summer / monsoon | Jul–Aug | 25–40 | 15–25 | 15–25 | 30–50 | 25–40 | 25–40 | 20–35 | 20–35 | | Late summer / harvest | Sep | 20–30 | 15–25 | 15–20 | 20–30 | — | 15–25 | 20–30 | 20–30 | | Post-harvest | Oct–Nov | 8–12 | 8–12 | 8–12 | 10–15 | 10–15 | 10–15 | 10–15 | 10–15 | These numbers assume a mature tree (year 5+) on a Dragoon site with 15 minutes of irrigation = roughly 1 gallon per emitter. Adjust downward for monsoon weeks (8" of August rain in two weeks reduces irrigation need by half or more) and upward for unusually dry windy springs. Deep-and-infrequent beats shallow-and-frequent. A mature orchard tree wants one long deep soak every 7–10 days in summer, not a 15-minute drip every other day. The deep soak encourages roots to follow the water down, which builds drought tolerance and tree health. The frequent shallow watering pattern that works for vegetables is wrong for trees.

sh: Irrigation for the orchard — 4 Mulch and weed pressure
b: Bare soil under a fruit tree heats to 130 °F in Cochise sun, dries fast, and grows aggressive weeds (bindweed, Russian thistle, prickly lettuce) that compete for water. Mulch every tree: First year: 4" of straw or wood-chip mulch out to 24" radius. Keep mulch 4" away from the trunk to prevent crown rot and rodent damage. Year 2+: wood-chip mulch out to the dripline; refresh annually. Gravel option: decomposed granite or pea gravel mulch (1/2"–3/4") works for figs, pomegranates, and jujubes and lasts 5+ years. Less effective for stone fruit which prefer organic mulch. A mulched tree uses 30–40 % less water than a bare-soil tree and is easier to weed-control.

sh: Irrigation for the orchard — 5 Winter watering
b: Don't stop watering in the dormant months — just water much less. A deep soak (the tree's full week-of-summer volume) once per month from December through February prevents the desiccation that kills marginal trees in dry Dragoons winters. For the same reason discussed in Guide 5 §8 (post-harvest vine care), dormant trees are still actively translocating water and the root system is alive; it just looks dead from above.

sh: Annual orchard calendar
b: Month-by-month maintenance schedule for the whole orchard | Month | Climate | Tasks | Watch points | | January | Cold, 3–6 hard-freeze nights; dormant | Dormant pruning all trees (figs, poms, jujubes, stone fruit, apples, pears). First dormant copper spray on peaches/nectarines. Plant bareroot trees. Winter water deep, monthly. Order any spring supplies. | Hard freeze January 5–15. Wrap young trunks if not done. | | February | Cold continuing; bud swell on stone fruit | Second dormant copper spray on peaches. Finish pruning by month end. Apricot/almond bloom may begin late month — frost-watch begins. Install frost-cloth caches near doors. Refresh white trunk paint on stone fruit. | First apricot/almond bloom triggers frost protection. Forecast watching becomes daily. | | March | Warming; bloom for most stone fruit and apples | Frost protection — drape cloth on cold-forecast nights. Bud-break fertilization — compost or fish emulsion drench at the dripline. Start drip irrigation after first warm week. Plant any remaining bareroot trees. Last frost typically Mar 25–Apr 5. | Peach/plum bloom in early-mid month. Pear and apple bloom late month. Frost-watch continues. | | April | Warm days, cool nights; fruit set | Fruit set — first small green fruits visible. Continue frost watch on cold forecasts. Thin apples when 1/2" diameter (4–6 weeks after bloom). Increase irrigation as canopy expands. Begin codling-moth trap monitoring for apples and pears. | Late frost still possible…

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management
b: Peach leaf curl · codling moth · borers · gophers · birds · javelina The Dragoons have lower fungal disease pressure than wetter fruit regions, but enough that two diseases (peach leaf curl and fireblight) and several insect pests need active management. Wildlife pressure is higher than in most fruit regions, and the orchard fence is not optional.

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 1 Disease summary
b: | Disease | Affected | Season | Symptoms | Control | | Peach leaf curl (Taphrina deformans) | Peach, nectarine | Late winter–spring | Red puckered curled leaves; defoliation | Dormant copper spray Jan + Feb. Plant Frost variety. | | Brown rot (Monilinia) | All stone fruit | Bloom + harvest | Blossom blight; fruit rot | Copper at bloom; sanitation; thin for airflow | | Fireblight (Erwinia amylovora) | Pear, apple | Spring (warm wet) | Shepherd's-crook blackened tips | Prune 12" below visible damage; sterilize between cuts; plant resistant varieties | | Bacterial canker | Stone fruit | Cool wet | Sunken bark lesions, gummosis | Avoid wet-weather pruning; copper | | Apple scab (Venturia) | Apple | Spring (wet) | Olive spots on leaves and fruit | Plant resistant varieties (Liberty); rake leaves; rare in Dragoons | | Powdery mildew | Apple, peach | Late summer humidity | White coating on leaves | Sulphur spray every 14 days from June; rare here | | Iron / zinc chlorosis | All trees on alkaline soil | Year-round | Interveinal yellowing of new growth | Foliar chelated iron + zinc; see Guide 4 Part 2 |

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 2 Insect pest summary
b: | Pest | Affected | Season | Symptoms | Control | | Codling moth (Cydia pomonella) | Apple, pear, walnut | May–Aug | Wormy fruit; entry hole at calyx | Pheromone traps + bagging + spinosad at first egg-hatch | | Peach tree borer (Synanthedon) | Peach, plum, almond | Jun–Aug egg-lay | Gummy frass at base of trunk | White trunk paint; pheromone traps; sanitation | | Green peach aphid | Peach, plum | Spring | Curled leaves, honeydew | Water spray; insecticidal soap; encourage ladybugs | | Spider mites | All trees | Hot dry | Stippled bronze leaves | Sulphur dust; reduce dust; encourage predator mites | | Apple maggot | Apple | Mid-late summer | Tunneled fruit; brown trails | Red sticky sphere traps; sanitation | | Scale insects | Citrus, occasionally stone fruit | Year-round | Bumpy crust on bark; honeydew | Horticultural oil in dormant season; predatory ladybugs | | Thrips | All trees, especially apricot | Spring | Silvery scarred fruit | Tolerate; spinosad if severe |

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 3 Wildlife management
Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 3 Wildlife management — Birds
b: Bird damage to stone fruit and figs can be severe — flocks of mockingbirds and curve-billed thrashers know when peaches ripen and return daily. The defenses: Netting — physical bird netting (3/4" mesh) draped over the tree from veraison (first color change) to harvest. Most effective; tedious to install on large trees. Harvest just before peak — pick fruit when it's still firm enough to ripen indoors. The window is narrow but reduces in-tree damage. Reflective tape, fake owls — minor effect; birds habituate within 1–2 weeks. Closed-eye fig varieties (Celeste) — bird-resistant compared to open-eye varieties (Mission, Brown Turkey). For figs specifically, the "harvest first" strategy works: visit the tree daily during ripening, picking fruit at the just-ripe stage before birds find it. A tree that has been routinely cleaned daily often loses very little fruit; a tree that's left alone for three days loses 30 % to birds in one bad weekend.

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 3 Wildlife management — Javelina
b: Javelina (collared peccary, Pecari tajacu) are the largest predictable threat to a Dragoons orchard. A 30 lb javelina will push over a small tree, strip ripe pomegranates, eat fallen figs, and chew bark from young trunks for the moisture. They are nocturnal, work in herds of 6–15, and cover surprising distances. The standard defense: Welded-wire fence, 48" high, around the entire orchard perimeter. Stake every 6 ft. Trunk wraps (corrugated drain pipe, milk-carton sleeves, or commercial vine guards) on the lower 24" of every young tree for the first 3 years. Don't leave fallen fruit on the ground — pick it up daily during ripening. Don't compost fruit waste at the orchard edge — javelina find the compost pile and stay. The unfenced backyard orchard is a javelina restaurant. Plant the fence before the trees. A herd that learns where the figs are will return every year, and once the orchard is in the local rotation it's much harder to drive them away than to keep them out the first time.

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 3 Wildlife management — Deer
b: Deer pressure varies hugely across the Dragoons — some sites see none, others see herds nightly. Where deer are present, an 8 ft fence is the only reliable defense. Motion-activated sprinklers and scare devices have short-lived effect; deer habituate. Solitary trees in fenced gardens can use individual welded-wire tree cages 6 ft tall.

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 3 Wildlife management — Gophers
b: Pocket gophers eat tree roots from below and can kill a young tree in a single season. Defenses are at planting time: Wire baskets — 1/2" galvanized hardware cloth fashioned into a 24" cube around the rootball. Active trapping — Macabee or Cinch traps set in fresh mounds when gophers are detected. No baits in orchard soil — secondary poisoning of hawks and owls is a real concern, and the local raptor population is part of the long-term gopher control.

sh: Pest, disease, and wildlife management — 3 Wildlife management — Squirrels and pack rats
b: Squirrels and pack rats take almonds, soft fruit, and young bark. Pack rats are particularly destructive — they build large stick nests at the base of trees and chew bark. Trap pack rats with snap traps baited with peanut butter; squirrels are harder to control and most orchards simply tolerate some loss.

sh: Harvest, storage, and use
b: Fig harvest window · pomegranate storage · drying stone fruit · cider and preservation The orchard year culminates in 4–5 months of harvest, from breba figs in June through Pink Lady apples in November. The Dragoons climate is unusually friendly to the post-harvest stage: low humidity makes drying fruit trivial, dry afternoon air carries away moisture from spread cuttings and racks, and the cool nights stretch the storage life of fall apples and pomegranates.

sh: Harvest, storage, and use — 1 Harvest indicators by crop
b: | Crop | Ready when | Storage life | Notes | | Fig | Hangs from stem; soft neck; full color; "honey drop" at eye | 24–48 h fresh; freezes well | Does not ripen off-tree. Pick daily. | | Pomegranate | "Tink" sound when tapped; flat at calyx | 2 months at 50 °F, 80% RH | Cut, don't pull. Pick before hard freeze. | | Jujube | Yellow-green with brown speckling (fresh) or brown wrinkled (dried) | Fresh 1–2 weeks; dried 1+ year | Wide window; can dry on tree | | Peach | Background color shifts yellow; fragrant; slight give | 5–10 days fresh; cans/freezes well | Picked firm-ripe stores best | | Apricot | Full orange; slight give; aromatic | 3–7 days fresh; dries beautifully | Ripens fast — harvest daily | | Plum (Japanese) | Background color shifts to mature color; slight give | 1–2 weeks fresh | Brix increases on-tree only | | Plum (European) | Color full; some softening at stem end | 2–4 weeks fresh; dries to prune | Allows longer hang time | | Almond | Hull cracks open along suture; nut rattles inside | 1+ year in shell | Dry on porch screen 2 weeks before storing | | Apple | Brown seeds; easy stem separation; flavor | 2–6 months at 32–40 °F, 90% RH | Pink Lady stores longest; pick at full color | | Pear (European) | Background green→yellow; firm at pick | 1–2 weeks fresh after ripening at room temp | Pick firm, ripen off-tree | | Pear (Asian) | Full color; sweet aroma; sample-test | 2–4 weeks fresh, refrigerated | Pick ripe on tree — Asian pears ripen on tree |

sh: Harvest, storage, and use — 2 Drying — the Dragoons advantage
b: Cochise County's low summer humidity (15–30 % outside monsoon) makes drying fruit easier here than anywhere east of California. A simple drying setup: Spread sliced fruit on stainless or food-safe nylon screens Stack screens on the covered porch in afternoon shade Air-dry 3–7 days depending on fruit and weather Finish in a dehydrator at 135 °F for 4–8 hours to ensure shelf-stable moisture content (< 15 %) What dries well: | Fruit | Drying notes | | Figs | Halve and air-dry. The Mediterranean classic. Mission and Brown Turkey both excellent. | | Apricots | Halve, pit, dry skin-down. Bright orange when finished. | | Peaches | Slice 1/4" thick, dip in lemon water to prevent browning. | | Plums (European) | Whole, with a small slit. Stanley and French Improved make traditional prunes. | | Apples | Slice 1/8" thick, dip in lemon water. Pink Lady and Granny Smith dry well. | | Jujubes | Often dry on the tree; finish on a screen for shelf storage. | | Pomegranate arils | Spread loose; brittle when fully dry. |

sh: Harvest, storage, and use — 3 Cider, juice, and preservation
b: A small backyard orchard easily produces more apples and pears than a household can eat fresh. The standard preservation directions: Apple cider — press in late October–November (when Granny Smith and Pink Lady are heavy). A small basket press handles a 10–20 tree backyard. Cider freezes well (gallon containers, 4" of headspace); fermented to hard cider with cider yeast; canned as sweet cider with a hot-fill at 185 °F. Pear preserves — Asian pears are best preserved as halves in light syrup; European pears as butter or as the classic pear-honey conserve. Stone fruit canning — peaches and apricots in light syrup are the most useful canning project of the year. Plan to do 30–50 quarts in August. Frozen fruit — sliced peaches and apricots tossed with a little sugar and lemon, flash-frozen on trays, then bagged. The simplest preservation; quality is excellent. Pomegranate juice — extract by halving and pressing, or by rolling whole fruit and tapping the juice. Freezes well in pints. Fruit leather — surplus apricots and peaches into a blender; spread on parchment; dehydrate at 135 °F until pliable. A planned Harvest & Preservation guide (Guide 12, not yet written) will go deeper on the cider, drying, and canning workflows. For now, the references to start with are the Ball Blue Book of Preserving (canning) and the UC Davis food preservation extension sheets (drying and fermentation).

sh: Harvest, storage, and use — 4 Storage temperatures
b: | Fruit | Optimal temp | Optimal humidity | Approx storage life | | Apple | 32–34 °F | 90–95 % | 2–6 months (variety-dependent) | | Asian pear | 32–34 °F | 90 % | 2–4 weeks | | European pear | 30–32 °F | 85–90 % | 1–3 months (then ripen at room temp) | | Pomegranate | 45–50 °F | 80–85 % | 2 months whole; 5 days arils | | Quince | 32–40 °F | 85 % | 2 months | | Jujube (dried) | 50–70 °F | < 50 % | 1+ year | | Almond (in shell) | 50–60 °F | < 60 % | 1+ year | A basement cold room or an old refrigerator dedicated to fruit (set to 35 °F, with a pan of water for humidity) gives the household the cool-storage capacity of a 1920s farmhouse cellar — apples in November can be eaten in March.

sh: Sources & references
b: Reference material consulted, suggested for further reading, and where to verify or extend the claims in this guide.

sh: Sources & references — Local resources
b: UA Cooperative Extension — Cochise County office (Willcox / Sierra Vista) — soil testing, fruit-tree variety advice, IPM and pest-identification help, and the seasonal bareroot tree sale. Phone 520-458-8278. UA Cooperative Extension Master Gardener program — Cochise County help desk; the right place to bring a sample of leaf-curl peach or a fireblight branch for diagnosis. Native Seeds/SEARCH (Tucson) — regional source for heritage and Southwest-adapted fruit varieties. Apple Annie's Orchard (Willcox) — the regional commercial apple operation; useful for variety reality-checks and harvest timing observations. Sulphur Springs Valley feed and farm supply — frost cloth, drip irrigation parts, gopher cages, copper fungicide.

sh: Sources & references — Books & references
b: Growing Food in a Hotter, Drier Land by Gary Paul Nabhan — the Sonoran orchard context behind the fig/pomegranate/jujube emphasis in Chapter 4. The Holistic Orchard by Michael Phillips — comprehensive low-input orchard management; the dormant-copper and trunk-paint protocols. The Apple Grower by Michael Phillips — the deep apple reference; codling moth IPM and pruning logic. Backyard Fruits and Berries by Diane Relf et al. — the household-scale planning framework. Western Garden Book (Sunset Publishing) — variety adaptation by zone, with detailed Southwest entries.

sh: Sources & references — Online resources
b: UA Cooperative Extension publications (extension.arizona.edu) — Backyard Fruit Tree Production in Arizona, chill-hour bulletins, peach leaf curl, and fireblight management sheets. UC Davis Fruit & Nut Research and Information Center (fruitsandnuts.ucdavis.edu) — the canonical rootstock, variety, and pest reference; chill-hour databases for nearly every cultivar. Stark Bro's Nursery (starkbros.com) — bareroot variety descriptions with reliable chill-hour data; the standard mail-order source for Cochise County. Trees of Antiquity (treesofantiquity.com) — heirloom apple and stone-fruit varieties; rootstock-specific orders. Bay Laurel Nursery (baylaurelnursery.com) — California bareroot specialist; reliable shipping to Cochise County. Womack Nursery (Texas) (womacknursery.com) — Southwest-adapted fig, pomegranate, jujube, and stone-fruit varieties. OMRI (Organic Materials Review Institute) (omri.org) — verify copper, spinosad, kaolin clay, and other orchard inputs before applying.

sh: Closing
b: A backyard orchard is a multi-year promise. The first fig planted on a January weekend is just a stick in the ground for the first eight weeks, leafs out hesitantly in April, sends up its first slender shoots in May, and produces maybe two figs the first August. The second August, it produces twenty. The third August, eighty. By the fifth August, it is producing 200 figs in a wave that lasts three weeks, and the household has more figs than it knows what to do with, and the freezer is filling with fig jam and the dehydrator is running every afternoon and the wasps and birds are competing with the family for the daily harvest, and an adolescent who refused fresh figs at year one is now eating them by the bowl. That is the rhythm of orchard work in the Dragoons. Plant slowly, plant for the long view, and let each tree mature on its own schedule. The first three years are an investment; the next thirty years are the dividend. The pattern that works for a Dragoon backyard orchard is something like this. In year one, plant a Black Mission fig, a Wonderful pomegranate, and a Li jujube. They are the easy three — heat-tolerant, drought-tolerant, low-pest, and almost impossible to kill once established. In year two, add a Red Haven peach and a Stanley plum, with the frost-cloth and dormant-copper-spray infrastructure to support them. In year three, add a Pink Lady apple and a Shinseiki pear, paired with a Granny Smith and a Hosui for pollination. In year four, fill in the gaps — a…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: How to use this guide
b: This guide is built as a reference. Almost no one reads a pest manual cover-to-cover. The way to use it is to know what's in it, scan the table of contents when something shows up in the bed, and read the entry for that one problem. The structure is in three parts: The framework (chapters 1–3). What IPM means, what makes the Dragoons different from other gardens, how to scout, when to act, when to leave it alone. The pest catalog (chapters 4–11). Insects sorted by feeding type (sucking, chewing, soil-dwelling, mites), then diseases by pathogen type (fungal, bacterial, viral), then the physiological disorders that look like disease but aren't. The vertebrate pressure (chapters 12–17). Small mammals, large mammals, birds, the reptile allies, dogs as security, the fencing reference. The action references (chapters 18–20). Beneficial insects and how to feed them, a spray decision tree, and a month-by-month scouting calendar. The Dragoons have a smaller pest community than most of the United States but a more unusual one. The dry climate kills most fungal disease. Mild winters fail to knock back pest populations the way northern winters do. Low-elevation desert species — javelina, packrat, harlequin bug, glassy-winged sharpshooter — pressure the garden in ways the average national gardening book completely fails to address. Anything in this guide that says "the Dragoons signature pest" is something you will not find ranked the same way in a generic reference. If you're new here…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: The IPM mindset
b: Prevention · scouting · biological controls · targeted intervention Integrated Pest Management (IPM) is the working philosophy this guide is built around. The short version: the spray bottle is the last tool, not the first. Most pest problems in the Dragoons are solved before they happen — by site choice, variety choice, planting date, row cover, sanitation, irrigation discipline, and habitat for beneficial insects. When all of those fail, then you intervene, and you intervene precisely. The four-step IPM logic: Prevention. Right crop, right variety, right timing, right place. A Frost peach (genetically resistant to leaf curl) planted on a sheltered south-east aspect is a different pest profile than a generic peach planted in a frost pocket. Hydroponic Bato buckets on a fenced patio have a different pest community than the same crop in an open soil bed. Scouting. Walk the bed regularly. Look at growing tips, leaf undersides, the base of stems, the soil surface. Knowing what's present before it explodes is what makes targeted intervention work. Biological controls. Encourage beneficial insects (lacewings, parasitic wasps, syrphid flies, ladybugs) and the larger food web (snake, hawk, owl) that handle most of the pest pressure for free. Flowering insectary strips of cilantro, dill, yarrow, alyssum, and fennel are the single highest-return habitat intervention. Targeted intervention. When prevention and biology fail, intervene with the most specific tool first. Hand-pick before…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: The IPM mindset — 1 The "tolerable damage" mindset
b: The biggest single mental shift for new growers is accepting that a healthy garden has pests in it. A cabbage with three aphids on the outer leaf is not a problem. A cucumber vine with five flea-beetle pinholes per leaf is not a problem. A peach tree with two scab spots out of a hundred fruit is not a problem. The goal is not zero pests; the goal is a stable community where predator and prey hold each other in rough balance and the crop yields enough for the kitchen. Reaching for the spray bottle at the first aphid does three things that work against you over time: Kills the beneficial insects that were about to handle the aphid for free. Selects for spray-resistant pest populations. Trains you to over-react, which costs time, money, and crop in the long run. Action thresholds, not zero thresholds. Decide before you scout how much damage triggers intervention. A useful default for most crops: take action when the pest population reaches a density that's visibly slowing growth or damaging the harvested part of the plant. Below that, scout and wait. The threshold for a flea beetle on a mustard leaf you'll harvest in a week is much higher than the same beetle on an eggplant transplant whose true leaves haven't yet emerged.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: The IPM mindset — 2 What this guide is, and isn't
b: This is a reference for the home grower and the small market gardener working in the Dragoon Mountains region of Cochise County, Arizona — USDA zone 8a, about 4,700 ft elevation. It is not a commercial pesticide application manual; for licensed applicator material, the University of Arizona Cooperative Extension (Cochise County office) and the Arizona Department of Agriculture are the canonical references. It is also not a veterinary or wildlife-removal manual; for wildlife conflicts beyond exclusion (a sick coyote in the yard, a rattlesnake in the shed), contact Arizona Game and Fish. The content is biased toward organic and low-input methods — not for ideological reasons, but because they have a better long-term outcome in a small garden ecosystem. Where a synthetic option is materially more effective (e.g., copper for peach leaf curl, Bacillus thuringiensis for caterpillars), it is named.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: The Dragoons pest & wildlife landscape
b: What makes Cochise County unique A Pacific-Northwest gardening book and a New-England gardening book are both wrong about the Dragoons in slightly different ways. The pest community here is shaped by four conditions you don't find together anywhere east of the Mississippi: Mild winters. Coldest winter nights are typically 18–25 °F, with hard freezes lasting only 3–6 nights per year. This is not enough to kill the overwintering eggs and adults of most insect pests. Harlequin bug, leafhopper, aphid, and tomato hornworm pupae all overwinter successfully in the Dragoons, which means populations build year over year unless actively managed. Monsoon humidity. From early July through mid-September, the Dragoons receive 6–10 inches of rain in 60 days. Daily humidity that was 8 % in June becomes 60 %+ in late July, and night dew sets on every leaf. This is the only window for fungal disease — powdery mildew, Botrytis, downy mildew, late blight — and it's compressed. Six weeks of risk, sandwiched between nine months of bone-dry air. Low-elevation desert species. The Dragoons sit on the edge of the Sonoran and Chihuahuan deserts. Species adapted to those environments — javelina, packrat, jackrabbit, harlequin bug, blister beetle, cactus longhorn beetle, kissing bug — pressure the garden in a way the same crop in Connecticut never sees. Sky-island biodiversity. The Dragoons are a "sky island" — an isolated mountain range surrounded by desert. Bird, snake, raptor, and small-mammal…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: The Dragoons pest & wildlife landscape — 1 The Dragoons pest "fingerprint"
b: If you've gardened elsewhere, here's what's different. | Conditions you're used to | What the Dragoons do instead | | Winter kills overwintering pests | Mild winter — most pests overwinter successfully | | Slugs are the dominant ground pest | Slugs only matter for 6 weeks post-monsoon | | Squash bug is the worst cucurbit pest | Cucumber beetle is worse — bacterial wilt vector | | Cabbage looper is the worst brassica pest | Harlequin bug is worse — not affected by Bt | | Deer are the worst mammal pressure | Javelina match or exceed deer (and bypass low fences) | | Mice and voles are the worst rodent pressure | Packrat (woodrat) is bigger and more destructive | | Powdery mildew runs all summer | Powdery mildew has a 6-week monsoon window | | Damping-off is a year-round seedling risk | Damping-off is mostly a hydroponic / indoor problem | | Late blight kills tomatoes in August | Late blight is rare — too dry between rains | | Pierce's disease takes out vineyards | Pierce's disease is essentially absent above 4,500 ft | | Codling moth is a 2-generation pest | Codling moth runs 3 generations in the long warm fall |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: The Dragoons pest & wildlife landscape — 2 The "year-round" effect
b: In most American climates, January is a reset. Pest populations crash, gardeners regroup. In the Dragoons, January is a slightly slower version of October. Aphids are still on the overwintered kale. Harlequin bug adults are still under the mulch. Packrats are still in the woodpile. The implication: pest management is a 12-month routine, not a March-to-October push. Sanitation in the fall, dormant sprays in January, scouting in February — all of these matter more here than they do in colder country. The single most useful pest-management habit in the Dragoons is the dusk walk. Most pest insects feed in low light. Hornworms, squash bugs, harlequins, cucumber beetles, and cutworms are all easier to spot at dusk than at noon. A flashlight, ten minutes a day in season, no spray, picking by hand — this beats most chemical interventions on plots under a quarter-acre.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: IPM principles in practice
b: Scouting frequency · action thresholds · biological controls · "tolerable damage" This chapter is the operating manual for the framework in chapter 1. How often to scout, what to look for, what triggers action.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: IPM principles in practice — 1 Scouting frequency
b: | Crop / context | Scouting frequency | What to look for | | Brassica transplants (Aug–Oct) | Every 2 days | Cabbage white eggs (yellow, on leaf underside); harlequin bug adults; slug damage | | Cucurbits (Jul–Sep) | Every 2 days | Cucumber beetle adults; squash bug egg clusters (copper-colored, on leaf underside); wilt symptoms | | Tomato (Jul–Sep) | Every 3 days | Hornworm frass on leaves below feeding sites; psyllid yellow-stippling on new growth | | Peppers (May–Oct) | Weekly | Aphid clusters on growing tips; thrip silvery scarring on leaves | | Grape (May–Sep) | Weekly + storm-trigger | Powdery mildew on shaded canopy interior; sharpshooter activity | | Orchard (year-round) | Weekly in season, monthly in dormancy | Codling moth egg traps; trunk damage; gopher mounds | | Hydroponic reservoir | Daily | Water temperature; root color (white = clean, brown = Pythium); algae on walls | | Soil beds (winter) | Weekly | Packrat sign; aphid colonies on cool-season crops; cutworm damage to transplants | The schedule above assumes the bed is in active production. During dormancy or transition, weekly is fine.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: IPM principles in practice — 2 Action thresholds — when to intervene
b: The honest answer is: it depends on the crop and the pest. Some workable defaults: | Pest | Action threshold | Reasoning | | Aphids on growing tips | Visible curl of new growth, or 30+ per tip | Below this, predators usually catch up | | Spider mites | Visible stippling on >10 % of leaves | Mites explode quickly past this point | | Cucumber beetle | Any beetle, on any cucurbit | Vector for bacterial wilt — one bite can transmit | | Squash vine borer eggs | Any egg, on any squash | Single bore = dead vine; prevention only | | Cabbage white caterpillars | 1+ caterpillar per plant | Multiplies fast; Bt at first sign | | Harlequin bug | Any adult, anywhere on brassicas | Reproduces in days; act on first sighting | | Tomato hornworm | 1+ on a plant | Will defoliate; hand-pick on sight | | Codling moth | 5+ moths in pheromone trap per week | Bag fruit or spray spinosad | | Slugs | Multiple seedlings damaged overnight | Iron-phosphate bait or beer trap | | Cutworm | 1 transplant cut overnight | Cardboard collars on all remaining transplants | | Powdery mildew | Any spotting on monsoon-window crops | Already past prevention; spray immediately | | Peach leaf curl | Any symptom in spring | Too late; spray dormant next winter | The threshold logic is different by pest: anything that vectors disease (cucumber beetle, leafhopper, psyllid) or that builds populations explosively (mites, aphids, harlequin) gets a low threshold. Anything that's mostly cosmetic (flea beetle on mature leaves)…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: IPM principles in practice — 3 Biological controls — the food web
b: The Dragoons have an unusually rich beneficial-insect community when you stop spraying for it. The main categories: Parasitic wasps (Cotesia, Trichogramma, Aphidius) — tiny, mostly invisible, lay eggs in caterpillars and aphids. The mummified-aphid skins on a tomato plant are evidence of Aphidius. The grain-of-rice cocoons on a hornworm's back are Cotesia. Both attracted by small flowering plants (cilantro, dill, fennel, yarrow, sweet alyssum). Lacewings (Chrysoperla) — green adult; larvae are voracious aphid and mite predators. Ladybugs (Hippodamia convergens native; Harmonia axyridis invasive) — adults and larvae both eat aphids. The native species overwinters in cliff crevices in the Dragoons; you don't need to buy them. Syrphid flies (hover flies) — adults look like small bees; larvae are aphid predators. Predatory mites — invisible to the naked eye, eat spider mites. Killed indiscriminately by sulphur and broad-spectrum sprays. Assassin bugs, big-eyed bugs, minute pirate bugs — generalist predators of small soft insects. Spiders — the most underrated predator. Every spider in the garden is on your side. Beyond insects, the vertebrate predators matter: Gopher snake, kingsnake — rodent control, gopher control. See §15. Red-tailed hawk, great horned owl, kestrel — rodent and rabbit control. Roadrunner — eats lizards, large insects, even small snakes. Bats — moth control at night, including codling moth and corn earworm. The implication: a garden surrounded by native…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: IPM principles in practice — 4 Hydroponic vs soil — different pest profiles
b: The two production systems have different pest communities. Knowing the difference saves a lot of misdiagnosis. | Pest / problem | Soil bed | Hydroponic | | Cutworms | Yes — at transplant | No — irrelevant indoors / no soil | | Grasshoppers | Yes (drought years) | Only outdoor Bato buckets | | Slugs | Yes — post-monsoon | Rare; sometimes on rockwool | | Aphids, thrips, mites | Yes | Yes — same control | | Cucumber beetle | Yes | Yes if outdoor; rare indoors | | Squash vine borer | Yes | Rare — no soil for pupation | | Soil-borne fungal disease (Fusarium, Verticillium) | Yes | No — system can be flushed | | Root-knot nematode | Yes — old beds | No — sterile media | | Pythium root rot | Rare with good drainage | Yes — the #1 hydro threat | | Reservoir algae | n/a | Yes — cover reservoir | | Fungus gnats | Mostly with overwatering | Yes — rockwool transition | | Powdery mildew | Yes — monsoon | Yes — same biology | | Javelina, packrat, gopher | Yes | Only outdoor / unfenced | The single most important hydroponic-only pathogen is Pythium — see Guide 2 §8 and Guide 3 for the full reservoir-temperature protocol. The single most important soil-only pest is the cutworm at transplant.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects
b: Aphids · thrips · whitefly · leafhoppers · psyllid · mealybug · scale Sucking insects insert a piercing-sucking mouthpart into plant tissue and feed on sap. Damage shows as stippling, distortion, sticky honeydew (and the sooty mold that grows on it), curled leaves, and stunting. Several species also vector viruses. As a group, sucking insects respond to: water sprays (mechanical knockdown), insecticidal soap, neem oil, and the predators (lacewings, ladybugs, syrphid flies, parasitic wasps) that handle them invisibly when not sprayed.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects — 1 Aphids
b: There are roughly 5,000 aphid species globally; the Dragoons see about a dozen with any regularity. The four you'll meet: | Species | Color | Main hosts | Notes | | Green peach aphid (Myzus persicae) | Pale green | Peppers, peaches, brassicas, many | Vectors >100 plant viruses | | Cabbage aphid (Brevicoryne brassicae) | Gray-green, waxy | Brassicas only | Heavy waxy coat resists soap | | Cotton/melon aphid (Aphis gossypii) | Yellow-green | Cucurbits | Vectors cucumber mosaic | | Woolly apple aphid (Eriosoma lanigerum) | White fuzz on bark | Apple roots, branches | Hardest to control; serious in pomes | Identification. Dense clusters of small (1–3 mm) soft-bodied insects on growing tips, leaf undersides, and flower buds. Sticky honeydew below the colony. Ants tending the colony (farming aphids for honeydew). Sooty mold growing on honeydew. Life cycle. In the Dragoons mild winter, females can reproduce parthenogenetically (without mating, live birth) year-round on overwintered hosts (kale, garlic tops, peach buds). Populations explode in spring as new growth appears, crash in midsummer heat, rebound on fall brassicas. A single colony can double every 4–5 days. Control sequence. Hose blast. Water spray in the morning, focused on growing tips. Repeat every 3 days. Mechanically dislodges aphids, which mostly can't climb back up. Encourage predators. Plant cilantro, dill, yarrow nearby and let them flower. Don't spray. Insecticidal soap. Direct contact spray on visible colonies…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects — 2 Thrips
b: Thrips are tiny (1–2 mm) slender insects that rasp leaf surfaces and suck up the exuding sap. The two species that matter: Onion thrips (Thrips tabaci) — alliums primarily, also brassicas; silvery flecks on inner leaf curl. Western flower thrips (Frankliniella occidentalis) — peppers, tomatoes, flowers; vectors tomato spotted wilt virus (TSWV). Identification. Silvery, papery scars on leaves and fruit. Tiny black "frass dots" along with the silvering. The insects themselves are hard to see; tap a flower over a white sheet and watch for cream-colored slivers running across. Scouting. Yellow or blue sticky traps near flowering plants. Blue traps catch western flower thrips better than yellow. Control sequence. Reflective mulch. Aluminum-foil or silver-plastic mulch around plants disorients flying thrips. Most useful in commercial settings, marginally useful at home scale. Hose blast mornings on heavily infested plants. Spinosad — a soil-bacterium-derived insecticide effective on thrips. Spray at dusk; allows recovery time for pollinators before sunrise. Insecticidal soap for hot infestations on non-flowering plants.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects — 3 Whitefly
b: Tiny (1.5 mm) white moth-like insects that fly up in a cloud when a leaf is disturbed. The two species: Greenhouse whitefly (Trialeurodes vaporariorum) — common in indoor and protected production. Silverleaf / sweetpotato whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) — outdoor in warm regions; vectors several geminiviruses. Identification. White cloud rising from leaf undersides when disturbed. Yellow stippling on upper leaf. Honeydew and sooty mold below colony. Control sequence. Yellow sticky traps at canopy height — catches adults; primarily for monitoring. Insecticidal soap weekly until population breaks. Neem oil — disrupts whitefly egg development. Most effective tool. Encourage predators — Encarsia formosa (parasitic wasp) is the commercial biocontrol for whitefly; in the home garden, lacewings and predatory mites help.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects — 4 Leafhoppers and sharpshooters
b: Wedge-shaped jumping insects, 3–5 mm, that run sideways on stems and jump away when disturbed. They feed on plant sap and several species vector serious diseases. Beet leafhopper (Circulifer tenellus) — vectors curly top virus on tomatoes, beans, peppers, beets. Glassy-winged sharpshooter (Homalodisca vitripennis) — vectors Pierce's disease on grapes. Cold-limited; rare at Dragoons elevation (4,500+ ft) but present at lower Cochise sites. Potato leafhopper (Empoasca fabae) — causes "hopperburn" stippling and necrotic margins on beans, potatoes. Identification. White or yellow stippling on leaves. Insects that jump or fly when leaves are touched. Hopperburn shows as V-shaped tip yellowing followed by necrosis. Control sequence. Row cover at transplant on susceptible crops in years with high leafhopper pressure. Reflective mulch disorients them. Insecticidal soap — only effective on direct contact; spray when leafhoppers are visible. Pyrethrin if numbers are heavy and host crops are at risk. Dusk only. The bigger management is at the variety / planting-date level: curly-top-tolerant tomato varieties exist, and early planting helps the crop establish before peak leafhopper flight (May–June).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects — 5 Psyllid
b: Looks like a tiny cicada (2–3 mm). Adults jump or fly; nymphs are flattened scale-like discs that stay put on leaf undersides. Tomato/potato psyllid (Bactericera cockerelli) — vectors zebra chip in potato and "psyllid yellows" in tomato. Major Southwest pest in dry warm springs. Identification. Yellowed cupping of new growth on tomato and potato (the "yellows" symptom). Tiny flat nymphs (like a flat scale insect) on leaf undersides. Sticky honeydew with a granular white "psyllid sugar" on leaves. Control sequence. Row cover at transplant through May. Sulphur dust — disrupts psyllid populations; useful pre-bloom. Insecticidal soap — directly on nymphs. Tolerate. Most years, the Dragoons see moderate psyllid pressure that doesn't significantly affect yield.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects — 6 Mealybugs and scale
b: Less common in outdoor Dragoons gardens but persistent on citrus, indoor figs, hibiscus, and overwintered hydroponic plants. Mealybugs — cottony white masses in leaf axils. Treat with rubbing alcohol on a cotton swab, or repeated insecticidal-soap sprays. Persistent — don't expect a single application to fix it. Scale insects — bumpy crust on bark or stems, often along the underside of leaf midribs. Soft scale (sticky, exudes honeydew) and armored scale (no honeydew, harder shell). Dormant-season horticultural oil is the standard control. Encourage predatory ladybugs (the Lindorus / Rhyzobius species are key here).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Sucking insects — 7 Reference table — sucking insects at a glance
b: | Pest | Season | Crops | Severity | First-line control | | Green peach aphid | Oct–Apr | Peppers, brassicas, peach | Moderate | Hose blast | | Cabbage aphid | Nov–Feb | Brassicas | High in late fall | Hose blast + soap | | Cotton aphid | Jul–Sep | Cucurbits | Moderate | Predators, soap | | Woolly apple aphid | All season | Apple | Moderate | Dormant oil | | Onion thrips | Mar–May | Alliums | Low | Hose blast | | Western flower thrips | Apr–Sep | Peppers, tomato, flowers | Moderate | Spinosad | | Whitefly | Indoor / warm season | Indoor crops, tomato | Moderate | Soap + sticky traps | | Beet leafhopper | Apr–Jun | Tomato, bean (vectors curly top) | Year-dependent | Row cover | | Sharpshooter | Apr–Sep | Grape (vectors Pierce's) | Low at elevation | Tolerate | | Tomato/potato psyllid | Apr–Jun | Tomato, potato | Moderate | Row cover, sulphur | | Mealybug | Year-round indoors | Citrus, indoor crops | Low-moderate | Alcohol swab, soap | | Scale | Year-round | Citrus, stone fruit | Low-moderate | Dormant oil |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects
b: Caterpillars · beetles · borers · grasshoppers Chewing insects remove plant tissue with mandibles. Damage shows as missing tissue (holes, notches, defoliation), frass (insect droppings), and sometimes stem severing or fruit boring. Many can be hand-picked, which is the highest-return single tool for this group on small plots. Where spraying is needed, Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) — a soil bacterium toxic to caterpillars but harmless to bees, beneficials, mammals, and birds — is the canonical first reach for caterpillar pests. Bt does not affect beetles or true bugs.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 1 Cabbage white & cabbage looper
b: The two most common brassica caterpillars in the Dragoons. Cabbage white butterfly (Pieris rapae) — small white butterfly with one to two black spots per wing. Lays single yellow eggs (1 mm, bullet-shaped) on the underside of brassica leaves. Larvae are bright green and velvety, growing to about 30 mm. Cabbage looper (Trichoplusia ni) — adult is a nondescript brown moth; larva is similar to the cabbage white green caterpillar but moves in a looping "inchworm" posture and is more nocturnal. Crops affected. All brassicas (cabbage, broccoli, cauliflower, kale, brussels sprouts, kohlrabi, collards, mustards, arugula). Life cycle in the Dragoons. Adults appear in mid-August as fall brassica transplants go in. Eggs hatch in 4–8 days; larvae feed 2–3 weeks before pupating. Two to three generations per fall. Cabbage white overwinters as pupa attached to fences, walls, and dry brush; cabbage looper overwinters in southern Arizona and migrates north each spring. Scouting. Look for yellow eggs on leaf undersides, ragged holes in leaves (start at outer leaves, work toward heart), dark green frass pellets on lower leaves. Control sequence. Floating row cover at transplant through about 4 weeks. Cover edges with soil — butterflies will lay through any gap. Bt kurstaki (Bacillus thuringiensis subsp. kurstaki) — spray weekly once cover comes off. Apply at dusk; Bt breaks down in UV within 24 hours. Hand picking — for small plantings, 10 minutes a week per 50 sq ft is sufficient. Spinosad…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 2 Hornworms
b: Two related species — the tomato hornworm (Manduca quinquemaculata) and the tobacco hornworm (Manduca sexta) — feed on Solanaceae (tomato, pepper, eggplant, potato). The tobacco hornworm is more common in the Southwest. Both are large (up to 100 mm), green, with a curved spine ("horn") on the rear. Identification. Large green caterpillars on tomato or pepper foliage. Sudden defoliation of a single stem overnight. Pellet-sized green-black frass on lower leaves. The caterpillar itself is famously hard to see — its color matches the leaf exactly — but you can usually spot the frass first, then look up to find the larva. Life cycle. Adult is a large gray-brown "hawk moth" that hovers like a hummingbird at evening flowers. Eggs are laid on leaf undersides. Larvae feed 3–4 weeks, then drop and pupate in soil. In the Dragoons, peak hornworm pressure is mid-July through early September during the monsoon. Control sequence. Daily dusk scouting during monsoon — flashlight, walk the bed, look for frass, then look up. Hand-pick into a jar. Bt kurstaki spray on heavy infestations. Leave parasitized larvae alone. A hornworm with grain-of-rice-sized white cocoons on its back has been parasitized by Cotesia congregata — a parasitic wasp. Don't kill it; let the wasps emerge to attack other hornworms. Trichogramma wasps — commercial release for serious infestations; targets eggs before they hatch.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 3 Harlequin bug — THE Dragoons brassica pest
b: The signature local brassica pest, and one of the few that's worse in the Dragoons than in most of the country. Murgantia histrionica — bright orange and black shield-shaped true bug, 8–12 mm. Adults overwinter in plant debris and emerge in late summer to attack fall brassicas. Harlequin bug is THE Dragoons brassica pest. It is not affected by Bt. It is hard to kill once established. It is the single biggest reason fall brassica plantings fail in Cochise County. Plan exclusion (row cover + sanitation) into the brassica calendar — before the bug appears, not after. Identification. Vivid orange-and-black shield-shaped bugs on brassica foliage. Yellow-and-white stippling on leaves (sap drained from feeding sites). Eggs laid in tidy double rows of barrel-shaped, black-and-white-striped eggs on leaf undersides. Nymphs are wingless miniature versions of adults. Crops affected. All brassicas, with mustards, kale, and turnip especially favored. Will also feed on tomato, bean, and corn in late season when brassicas are limited. Life cycle. Three or four generations per year in southern Arizona. Adults overwinter under rocks, in leaf litter, in dead brassica stalks. Emergence in late August lines up exactly with fall brassica transplant. A pair can produce 100+ eggs. Scouting. Daily inspection from August 15 onward. Look for adults sunning on top of leaves in cool morning. Look for egg rows on leaf undersides. Look for stippled feeding damage. Control sequence. Sanitation in spring…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 4 Cucumber beetle — the cucurbit vector
b: The Dragoons signature cucurbit pest, and the most important warm-season pest in the whole guide because of its disease-vector role. See Guide 7 §5.5 for the full protocol; what follows is the reference summary. Two species both present: Striped cucumber beetle (Acalymma vittatum) — yellow with three black stripes. Spotted cucumber beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) — yellow with twelve black spots. Cucumber beetle = bacterial wilt vector. The beetle by itself eats a small amount of foliage and flowers — annoying but not crop-threatening. The reason it's the signature cucurbit pest is that it vectors Erwinia tracheiphila — bacterial wilt — which has no cure. One bite from an infected beetle can kill a mature cucumber vine in 7–14 days. This is why the threshold is "any beetle, anywhere on a cucurbit" rather than the usual population threshold. Identification. Small (5–7 mm) yellow beetles with stripes or spots on cucumber, squash, melon, or zucchini foliage and flowers. Wilting of individual stems, then whole plant, despite adequate moisture — usually the bacterial-wilt signal. Life cycle. Adults overwinter in leaf litter at the edge of fields, emerge in late June, peak after the first big monsoon rain in early July. Two to three generations per year. The first generation is the dangerous one for bacterial wilt — the beetles emerge carrying the bacterium from last year's overwintering sites. Control sequence. (See Guide 7 §5.5 for full protocol.) Row cover from transplant…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 5 Squash vine borer
b: A clearwing moth (Melittia cucurbitae) whose larva bores into squash stems and kills the plant from inside. The biggest single-plant killer in the Dragoons cucurbit patch. See Guide 7 §6.4 for the full protocol. Identification. Sudden wilt of an entire squash or zucchini plant despite full moisture. Frass (sawdust-like) at the base of the main stem. Slicing the stem lengthwise reveals a fat cream-colored caterpillar tunneling inside. Life cycle. Single adult emergence in mid-to-late July in the Dragoons. Adult resembles a wasp (orange and black, day-flying). Lays eggs at base of squash stems. Larvae hatch and immediately bore in; the damage is done before it's visible. Control sequence. Aluminum foil collar wrapped around the base of every squash and zucchini stem from May through August. Physically blocks egg-lay. Single highest-return intervention. Second sowing in late July as insurance — even if the first planting collapses, the second will produce in September after borer flight ends. Stem injection of Bt with a syringe at the first sign of frass — sometimes saves a plant. Sanitation. Remove and burn (or trash) all squash debris in fall.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 6 Corn earworm
b: The single most reliable pest of sweet corn in the Dragoons. Helicoverpa zea — the larva of a brown night-flying moth. Same species attacks tomato (tomato fruitworm), cotton (cotton bollworm), and bean (bean podworm). Identification. Cream to brown caterpillars (up to 35 mm) inside the ear tips, eating kernels. Frass at the silks. The ear tip is the only part affected — kernels below the feeding zone are usually fine. Life cycle. Adult moth lays single eggs on fresh corn silks. Larva crawls down the silk into the ear tip. Three or four generations per year in southern Arizona; peak August. Control sequence. Plant early — April 15 if soil temperature allows. The first corn flush gets the lightest pressure. Resistant varieties with tight husks (Country Gentleman, Silver Queen) — bird and bug resistance both. Mineral oil drop — a few drops of mineral oil mixed with Bt onto fresh silks, 6 days after silking. Suffocates and kills early larvae. Tolerate the tip damage. A 1-inch tip with a worm can be cut off; the rest of the ear is perfect.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 7 Codling moth — the apple pest
b: Cydia pomonella. The dominant pest of apple and pear in the Dragoons. See Guide 9 §10 for the full orchard protocol. Identification. Wormy fruit — small caterpillars eating into the fruit, usually entering through the calyx (blossom) end. Frass at the entry hole. Apples drop prematurely. Life cycle. Three generations per year in the long Dragoons apple season — late April, late June, and late August. Adult is a small gray moth with bronze wing-tips; flies at dusk. Control sequence. Pheromone traps for monitoring. Threshold: 5+ moths per trap per week triggers spray. Bag fruit individually in paper or footed nylons at thumbnail size. Labor-intensive but effective for backyard trees with under 100 fruit. Spinosad spray at first egg-hatch (about 250 degree-days after first trap catch). Sanitation. Pick up dropped fruit weekly through summer. Codling moth granulosis virus (CYD-X) — commercial biocontrol effective for serious infestations.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 8 Flea beetle
b: Tiny (2 mm) shiny black beetles that jump when disturbed, leaving pinholes in leaves. Phyllotreta and Epitrix species. Multiple species attack different crops. Identification. Pinholes on leaves; tiny black beetles that jump away when leaves are touched. Worst on young seedlings; mature plants outgrow the damage. Crops affected. Eggplant primarily; mustards, arugula, and young brassicas secondary; tomato and sweet potato occasionally. Control sequence. Row cover at transplant through about 4 weeks of growth. Tolerate on established plants — cosmetic damage only. Nasturtiums as trap crop — flea beetles prefer them. Pyrethrin for severe seedling damage; dusk only.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 9 Blister beetle
b: Long (15–25 mm) slender beetles, often striped or black, that appear in swarms after monsoon rains. Epicauta species. Eat tomato, potato, pepper, and amaranth foliage. Notable because they contain cantharidin — a chemical that blisters human skin on contact. Identification. Sudden swarm of long beetles on tomato or pepper plants, often defoliating an entire plant in hours. Black, gray, or striped pattern. Control sequence. Hand-pick wearing gloves. Cantharidin causes blisters; do not touch beetles directly. Knock into a bucket of soapy water with a stick. Row cover on susceptible crops during monsoon for the worst years. Tolerate if the swarm is brief — they often move through in 1–3 days.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 10 Grasshoppers
b: In drought years, a serious pest of nearly everything. Three or four species in the Dragoons; Melanoplus species dominant. Identification. Rapid defoliation, no specific pattern. Visible grasshoppers, especially in late afternoon. Control sequence. Row cover during heavy years. Nosema locustae bait — protozoan biocontrol that infects grasshoppers; slow-acting and works only on nymphs. Apply early in season. Kaolin clay spray — coats foliage with a film that grasshoppers won't feed on. Hard to keep on through monsoon rain. Chickens or guinea fowl if you keep them — significant control.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Chewing insects — 11 Reference table — chewing insects
b: | Pest | Season | Crops | Severity | First-line control | | Cabbage white | Aug–Oct, Mar–May | Brassicas | Moderate | Row cover + Bt | | Cabbage looper | Sep–Nov | Brassicas | Moderate | Bt | | Harlequin bug | Sep–Nov | Brassicas | High — signature | Sanitation + row cover | | Tomato hornworm | Jul–Sep | Solanaceae | High | Dusk hand-pick | | Cucumber beetle | Jul–Sep | Cucurbits | High — signature | Exclusion + dusk spray | | Squash vine borer | Jul (1 flight) | Squash, zucchini | High | Foil collar | | Corn earworm | Aug | Sweet corn | High | Early planting, mineral oil | | Codling moth | May–Aug | Apple, pear | High | Bagging, spinosad | | Flea beetle | May, also Mar–May | Eggplant, mustards | Low-moderate | Row cover | | Blister beetle | Jul–Sep (post-monsoon) | Tomato, pepper, potato | Year-dependent | Hand-pick (gloves) | | Grasshopper | Aug–Sep (drought) | All | Year-dependent | Nosema, row cover |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Soil pests
b: Wireworm · cutworm · root-knot nematode · root maggot · grubs Soil-dwelling pests are the ones you can't scout for directly — you see the damage above ground and infer what's eating roots below. Most soil pest management is preventive: cover crops, rotation, solarization, and exclusion at transplant.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Soil pests — 1 Cutworm
b: Gray-brown caterpillars (15–40 mm) that curl into a "C" when disturbed. Agrotis and Peridroma species. Hide in soil during day, emerge at night, sever transplant stems at ground level. Identification. Seedlings or transplants cleanly cut off at soil level, lying intact on the ground in the morning. Sometimes the caterpillar is curled in the soil within 2 inches of the damaged plant. Crops affected. Brassica transplants, tomato transplants, lettuce, anything at the small-transplant stage in August–October. Spring transplants in March–April. Life cycle. Multiple species; some overwinter as larvae in soil, others as adult moths. Peak damage at transplant time in late summer. Control sequence. Cardboard collars. Toilet-paper-tube cylinder cut to 3 inches, pushed 1 inch into soil at every transplant. Single highest-return intervention. Cardboard biodegrades after harvest. Diatomaceous earth ring around each transplant. Bt drench at transplant — modest effect. Hand-pick at dusk — they emerge at low light.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Soil pests — 2 Wireworm
b: Hard, shiny, slender orange-yellow larvae (15–30 mm) of click beetles (Agriotes, Limonius). Bore into root crops, seed potatoes, and tubers. Identification. Slender brown tunnels in carrots, potatoes, sweet potatoes, and root crops generally. Wireworms themselves are found in soil during cultivation. Crops affected. Root crops primarily; corn and bean seedlings if heavy. Life cycle. Adults are click beetles. Larvae live 2–4 years in soil before pupating. Worse in beds recently converted from sod or pasture (where larvae lived in grass roots). Control sequence. Avoid planting root crops in newly converted beds for 2 years after sod removal. Trap with potato chunks buried 4 inches deep — dig up weekly and destroy wireworms. Beneficial nematodes (Steinernema feltiae) applied as drench in spring. Heavy rotation — never the same root crop in the same bed two years running.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Soil pests — 3 Root-knot nematode
b: Microscopic worms (Meloidogyne species) that infect roots and cause galls, stunting, and progressive decline. Persistent in beds once established. Identification. Stunted above-ground growth despite adequate water and nutrients. Roots show small to large galls (bumpy lumpy swellings) when dug. Worse on carrots, beets, tomatoes, peppers, melons. Crops affected. Carrots and root crops show galls most dramatically; tomatoes, melons, and peppers are also susceptible. Brassicas, alliums, and corn are tolerant. Life cycle. Persistent in soil. One generation per 30–60 days during warm weather. Females lay 500+ eggs in gelatinous matrix attached to root. Control sequence. Rotation is everything. Never grow susceptible crops in the same bed two years running. Cover-crop with Nemagold marigolds (Tagetes patula) — 60-day cover crop produces alpha-terthienyl in roots, which suppresses nematode populations. Solarization. Clear plastic over moist soil for 6 weeks in midsummer. Works in the Dragoons sun; soil temps under plastic reach 140 °F. Soil amendments with crab/shrimp shell meal — chitin amendments encourage chitinase-producing soil microbes that attack nematode eggs. Plant resistant varieties. Tomato varieties marked "VFN" or "N" have nematode resistance.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Soil pests — 4 Root maggot
b: Delia species — the larvae of small flies that lay eggs at the soil line on brassica and onion stems. Larvae tunnel into roots and bulbs. Cabbage maggot (Delia radicum) — brassica roots and stem base. Onion maggot (Delia antiqua) — onion and garlic bulbs. Identification. Wilting brassica transplant without obvious above-ground cause; pull up and find white tunneling larvae at the root crown. Onions with brown rotted-out bulb interiors and tunneling. Control sequence. Row cover at transplant prevents adult egg-lay. Single most effective intervention. Wood-ash or diatomaceous earth ring around plant stems. Beneficial nematodes (Steinernema feltiae) as soil drench. Delay planting if a maggot outbreak is known in the area.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Soil pests — 5 White grubs
b: Larvae of June beetles, May beetles, and chafers. Large (15–30 mm), white, C-curled grubs in soil. Eat roots of grasses, sometimes seedlings. Identification. White C-shaped grubs found during cultivation. Usually a problem only in beds recently converted from lawn or in beds heavily amended with raw organic matter. Control sequence. Beneficial nematodes (Heterorhabditis bacteriophora) — soil drench in warm soil. Milky spore disease — bacterial control, slow to establish but persistent. Cultivation. Tilling exposes grubs to predator birds.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Soil pests — 6 Reference — soil pests
b: | Pest | Damage | Crops | Control sequence | | Cutworm | Severed transplants overnight | Brassicas, tomato | Cardboard collars | | Wireworm | Tunneled roots | Carrots, potatoes | Rotation, traps | | Root-knot nematode | Galled roots, stunting | Tomato, carrot, melon | Marigold cover, rotation, resistant varieties | | Cabbage maggot | Tunneled brassica roots | Brassicas | Row cover at transplant | | Onion maggot | Rotted onion bulbs | Alliums | Row cover at transplant | | White grubs | Root feeding | Lawn, seedlings | Beneficial nematodes |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Mites & non-insect pests
b: Spider mites · eriophyid mites · springtails Mites are not insects — they're arachnids, with eight legs as adults (six as larvae). Most are too small to see clearly without a hand lens. They reproduce explosively in dry hot weather and resist many conventional insecticides.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Mites & non-insect pests — 1 Spider mites
b: Two-spotted spider mite (Tetranychus urticae) is the dominant species. Adults are about 0.4 mm with two dark spots on the back. They feed on undersides of leaves and produce fine webbing. Crops affected. Almost everything — peppers, tomatoes, eggplant, beans, melons, hops, citrus, almost all hydroponic crops. Severe in dry heat (June pre-monsoon especially). Identification. Tiny stippled white-yellow flecking on leaves (each fleck is a feeding site). Fine webbing on leaf undersides and growing tips in heavy infestations. The mites themselves require a hand lens — look like moving specks of dust on the underside of a leaf, often near the midrib. Life cycle. Egg to adult in 5–7 days in hot dry weather. A single female lays 100+ eggs. Populations can double daily under perfect conditions (95 °F, low humidity). Overwinter as adult females in plant debris. Control sequence. Raise humidity. Mist plants in morning; mites hate humid microclimates. This is the highest-return single intervention in dry pre-monsoon June. Hose blast every 3 days on the undersides of leaves. Predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis, Neoseiulus californicus) — commercial release; effective in greenhouse and indoor production. Insecticidal soap weekly — contact only, needs direct hit on mites. Neem oil at dusk — effective and gentler than other options. Sulphur dust — effective on mites and powdery mildew simultaneously. Do not apply above 85 °F (burns foliage). Avoid broad-spectrum sprays. Pyrethrin kills…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Mites & non-insect pests — 2 Eriophyid mites
b: Microscopic (0.2 mm) mites that cause specific deformations rather than general stippling. Genus-specific: Tomato russet mite (Aculops lycopersici) — causes bronzing of lower tomato stems and leaves; severe in hot dry conditions. Grape erineum mite (Colomerus vitis) — causes raised galls (erineum patches) on grape leaves; cosmetic. Apple rust mite (Aculus schlechtendali) — causes russeting of apple skin; minor. Identification. Specific symptom by host — bronzed tomato stems, raised galls on grape, rusty apple skin. The mites themselves are not visible without strong magnification. Control sequence. Sulphur dust — effective. Same temperature caution as for spider mites. Tolerate cosmetic damage — erineum mites on grape, rust mites on apple — these rarely affect crop yield. Sanitation. Prune out heavily infested wood in dormancy.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Mites & non-insect pests — 3 Springtails
b: Tiny (1–3 mm) jumping arthropods that thrive in moist soil and decomposing organic matter. Not true insects, not true pests in most cases. Sometimes a nuisance in hydroponic rockwool and seedling trays. Control. Reduce moisture; springtails self-correct when conditions dry. No spray needed.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases
b: Powdery mildew · peach leaf curl · early blight · late blight · downy mildew · Fusarium · Verticillium · gray mold · Septoria · Pythium Fungal diseases in the Dragoons are seasonal — the dry air kills most fungi for nine months of the year, but the six-week monsoon window is a concentrated risk. The dominant principle for fungal disease is prevention before symptoms appear. Once you see disease, the season's outcome is mostly fixed; you can slow it but rarely stop it. Two times to act: dormant winter sprays before bud break (for peach leaf curl, brown rot), and preventive sprays in late June before the first monsoon rain (for powdery mildew, downy mildew, Botrytis).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 1 Powdery mildew
b: The most common fungal disease in the Dragoons. Multiple causal species — Podosphaera xanthii on cucurbits, Erysiphe species on others, Uncinula necator on grape. Identification. White powdery coating on upper leaf surfaces, sometimes shoots and stems. Affected leaves yellow and drop. Heavily affected fruit (cucumbers, melons) can develop sunburn from leaf loss.
