Crops affected. Squash, zucchini, cucumber, melon, grape, peppers (mild), peas, beans (late). Almost everything is susceptible to some species. Life cycle. Spores germinate at 60–80 °F under humid conditions but unlike most fungi do not require leaf wetness — they thrive in monsoon humidity even without rain. Peak July through September. Control sequence. Prevention via spacing and airflow. Trellis cucurbits vertically. Prune lower leaves off tomatoes. Open the canopy on grape vines before July 1. Resistant varieties. Many cucurbit varieties have powdery-mildew resistance (PMR) in their names. Use them. Potassium bicarbonate spray (Kaligreen, MilStop) every 14 days from late June. Best preventive. Milk spray — 1 part milk to 9 parts water, applied weekly. Genuine effect, well-documented in trials. Cheap. Sulphur — every 14 days from June. Do not apply above 95 °F. Effective on grape especially. Neem oil at dusk — moderate effect. The Dragoons powdery mildew onset is highly predictable. Look at three signals: (1) the first night dew on your beds — usually within a week of the first significant monsoon rain, (2)…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 2 Downy mildew
b: Different organism (oomycete, Peronospora and Pseudoperonospora species) than powdery mildew but similar appearance. Less common in the Dragoons because it needs continuous leaf wetness — which the dry-day-wet-night monsoon pattern doesn't always provide. Identification. Yellow angular patches on upper leaf, with a downy gray-purple fuzz on the underside (visible early morning when dew is fresh). Worse on cucurbits, brassicas, alliums. Control sequence. Same as powdery mildew, with copper-based sprays added for severe cases. Less common in the Dragoons than in humid climates.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 3 Peach leaf curl
b: The single most important stone-fruit disease in the Dragoons. Caused by Taphrina deformans. Infects buds during winter and emerges with the new spring leaves. Two dormant copper sprays, January and February. This is the single most important stone-fruit task in the Dragoon orchard calendar. Skip it and the peach crop is at risk. Do it religiously and peach leaf curl essentially never appears. Once symptoms appear in spring, control for that season is impossible. Identification. Red puckered, thickened, curled leaves on peach or nectarine in early spring (March–April). Severe defoliation. Following year, weakened tree with reduced bloom. Life cycle. Spores overwinter in bud scales. Spring rain triggers germination as buds swell. Once inside developing leaves, the fungus is untouchable. Control sequence. Mid-January copper spray at full label rate. Cover every twig and bud. Mid-February copper spray — repeat, especially if a wet spring is forecast. Plant resistant variety — Frost peach is genetically resistant; Indian Free moderately resistant. Sanitation. Rake and dispose of fallen leaves in autumn; do not compost on site. See Guide 9 §5 for the full stone-fruit protocol.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 4 Early blight
b: Caused by Alternaria solani. Most common on tomato; also on potato and eggplant. Identification. Concentric brown ring spots on lower leaves first; spots have a "bullseye" appearance. Yellowing and drop of affected leaves. Progresses up the plant. Life cycle. Overwinters on debris and in soil. Spreads by water splash. Peak during monsoon. Worse on stressed, low-nutrient plants. Control sequence. Mulch heavily to reduce soil splash onto lower leaves. Prune off lowest 12 inches of foliage on tomatoes. Drip irrigation, not overhead. Crop rotation — no tomatoes in the same bed for 3 years. Copper or chlorothalonil spray weekly during monsoon if disease appears early in the season. Resistant varieties (Iron Lady, Mountain Magic).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 5 Late blight
b: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Devastating in humid climates; rare in the Dragoons because the dry mid-day breaks the disease cycle. Identification. Olive-green or brown water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems. White fuzzy growth on undersides in humid weather. Fruit develops greasy brown lesions. Rapid death. Control. If late blight appears (rare): remove and destroy all infected plant material; copper or chlorothalonil spray as preventive. Plant in well-spaced sunny beds with airflow.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 6 Fusarium and Verticillium wilts
b: Two distinct soilborne fungi (Fusarium oxysporum and Verticillium dahliae) with similar symptoms. Both invade roots and plug vascular tissue. Identification. One-sided yellowing of lower leaves on tomato (Fusarium often), bilateral on eggplant (Verticillium often). Wilting progresses upward despite adequate water. Brown discoloration in stem vascular tissue when split. Life cycle. Persist 6+ years in soil. Spread by infected transplants, contaminated tools. Control sequence. Resistant varieties. "VF" (Verticillium + Fusarium resistant) or "VFN" (with nematode resistance) on tomato seed packets. Many modern hybrids. Long rotation — at least 4 years before susceptible crops return to an infected bed. Solarization of severely infested beds. No cure for infected plants — remove and trash.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 7 Gray mold (Botrytis)
b: Botrytis cinerea. The "noble rot" of wine grapes and the common cause of post-bloom decline on many fruits. Identification. Fuzzy gray mold on flowers, fruit, leaves. Worst on dense canopy fruit zones. Tomato fruit develops "ghost spot" rings; grape clusters get bunch rot. Crops affected. Strawberry, grape, tomato, peppers, beans (post-bloom), figs. Life cycle. Spores overwinter on debris. Cool wet conditions favor development — paradoxically less of an issue in hot midsummer than in cool monsoon mornings. Control sequence. Airflow. Open canopies, space plants, prune dense foliage. Mulch to keep splash off lower leaves. Sanitation. Remove dead flowers and damaged fruit before they sporulate. Copper or chlorothalonil preventive on grapes during monsoon bunch development.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 8 Septoria leaf spot
b: Septoria lycopersici on tomato primarily. Small dark spots with light centers on lower leaves, progressing upward. Control. Same as early blight — mulch, prune lower foliage, drip irrigation, rotation.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 9 Pythium root rot
b: The dominant hydroponic-system pathogen. Pythium species are oomycetes that attack roots in warm reservoirs (above 72 °F). Identification. Brown slimy roots in net pots; sour smell from reservoir; wilting despite full reservoir. Control sequence. See Guide 2 §8 and Guide 3 for full protocol. Drop reservoir temperature below 72 °F. Bury, insulate, shade. Flush system, replace media if roots are brown. Hydrogen peroxide at 3 ml/gal of 3% — dissolves bio-film, oxygenates root zone.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fungal diseases — 10 Other fungal issues
b: | Disease | Pathogen | Crops | Key control | | Apple scab | Venturia inaequalis | Apple | Resistant varieties, sanitation; rare in Dragoons | | Brown rot | Monilinia spp. | Stone fruit | Copper at bloom; thin for airflow | | Fire-blight (bacterial; see §9) | Erwinia amylovora | Apple, pear | Prune 12" below damage | | Anthracnose | Colletotrichum spp. | Tomato, pepper, beans | Sanitation, crop rotation | | Damping-off | Pythium, Rhizoctonia | Seedlings | Sterile seed-starting medium, airflow |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Bacterial diseases
b: Bacterial wilt · Pierce's disease · fireblight · bacterial speck/spot · soft rot Bacterial diseases are less common than fungal in the Dragoons but harder to control. Most have no chemical cure — the management is prevention (resistant varieties, vector control, sanitation) and removal of infected material.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Bacterial diseases — 1 Bacterial wilt of cucurbits
b: Erwinia tracheiphila. The reason cucumber beetle is the Dragoons signature cucurbit pest. Identification. Sudden wilt of a cucumber, melon, or squash vine despite adequate moisture. Cut a stem and a sticky stream of bacterial ooze pulls between the cut faces when separated. Death within 7–14 days of first symptoms. Crops affected. Cucumber and melon especially; squash and zucchini less commonly. Life cycle. Overwinters in cucumber beetle gut. Transmitted when beetle feeds on a healthy plant. Control. No cure once infected. Management is vector control — see §5.4 for cucumber beetle protocol, and Guide 7 §5.5 for the full cucurbit protocol.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Bacterial diseases — 2 Pierce's disease
b: Xylella fastidiosa. Bacterial disease of grapes and many other plants, vectored by xylem-feeding leafhoppers (sharpshooters). Identification. Marginal leaf scorch on grape (yellow to brown drying from the leaf edge inward, with a green-yellow border). Progressive vine decline over 2–5 years. Status in the Dragoons. Pierce's disease is essentially absent above 4,500 ft elevation. The bacterium does not survive Dragoons winter cold; vector populations are limited. The threat lies at lower-elevation Cochise sites. See Guide 5 §1 for the elevation-rule logic. Control. Site selection is everything — plant grapes above 4,500 ft. At lower elevations, vector control with kaolin clay and insecticidal soap helps, but the disease is functionally untreatable once established.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Bacterial diseases — 3 Fireblight
b: Erwinia amylovora. The dominant bacterial disease of apple and pear in the Dragoons. Moderate pressure here — drier than Pacific Northwest pear country but a real concern in monsoon years. Identification. "Shepherd's crook" — blackened bent tips on branches; shoot tips look scorched. Cankers (dark sunken bark patches) on larger branches. Bacterial ooze in warm wet weather. Crops affected. Apple, pear, and quince. Asian pears are much more resistant than European pears. Life cycle. Bacteria overwinter in cankers; spread by rain and insects during bloom. Worst in warm wet springs. Control sequence. Resistant varieties. Liberty, Pristine, Enterprise apples; Shinseiki, Hosui, Magness pears. Prune in dry weather. Cut 12 inches below visible damage; sterilize pruners between every cut (10 % bleach or 70 % alcohol). Avoid heavy nitrogen — succulent growth is more susceptible. Copper or streptomycin spray at bloom in severe-pressure years. See Guide 9 §7 for the full pome-fruit protocol.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Bacterial diseases — 4 Bacterial speck and spot
b: Pseudomonas syringae (speck) and Xanthomonas species (spot). Cool wet spring weather diseases on tomato and peppers. Identification. Small black spots with yellow halos on leaves and fruit. Fruit spots are small, dark, often scabby. Control. Disease-free seed — these bacteria spread on seed. Drip irrigation, not overhead. Copper spray weekly during cool wet spring. Crop rotation.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Bacterial diseases — 5 Soft rot
b: Erwinia carotovora and related species. Causes wet, foul-smelling rot of stored or wounded vegetables — onions, potatoes, cabbage heads, carrots, sweet peppers. Control. Prevention is everything: handle gently at harvest, cure properly, store dry. Once started, the rot spreads through the storage container.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Viral diseases
b: Tomato/tobacco mosaic · curly top · TSWV · cucumber mosaic Viral diseases have no cure. Management is vector control (the insect that carries the virus), resistant varieties, and removal of infected plants. All of them are common enough in the Dragoons that the home grower will encounter at least one in a typical season.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Viral diseases — 1 Tomato/tobacco mosaic virus (TMV/ToMV)
b: Mosaic-pattern yellowing on leaves; distorted growth. Vectored mechanically (on hands, tools, clothing — anyone who smokes tobacco can transfer ToMV from cigarette to plant). Persistent in soil. Control. Resistant varieties — most modern hybrid tomatoes carry the Tm-1 or Tm-2 resistance gene. Wash hands before handling tomatoes. Especially after smoking. Remove infected plants within 48 hours. Trash, don't compost. Sterilize tools with 10 % bleach between plants when pruning.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Viral diseases — 2 Curly top virus (BCTV)
b: Beet curly top virus. Vectored by the beet leafhopper. Year-dependent severity in the Dragoons — devastating in some years, absent in others. Identification. Stunted, upward-cupped leaves with yellowed veins. Plants stop growing and produce no fruit. Affects tomato, bean, beet, spinach, pepper. Control. Row cover at transplant in known bad years. Resistant varieties — fewer than for TMV, but some exist (Roza tomato is curly-top tolerant). Remove infected plants — they're not coming back. Plant earlier — establish the crop before the May–June peak of leafhopper migration.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Viral diseases — 3 Tomato spotted wilt virus (TSWV)
b: Vectored by western flower thrips. Causes a wide range of symptoms — bronzing of leaves, ringspots on fruit, stunting. Control. Resistant varieties. Most quality tomato hybrids now include "SW" (TSWV) resistance. Thrips control — reflective mulch, hose blast, spinosad. Remove infected plants within 48 hours.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Viral diseases — 4 Cucumber mosaic virus (CMV)
b: Vectored by aphids. Wide host range — cucurbits, peppers, tomatoes, ornamentals. Identification. Yellow mosaic pattern on leaves; distorted fruit (cucumber fruit becomes warty and bitter). Stunted growth. Control. Aphid control — see §4.1. Resistant varieties in cucumber (Marketmore 76, Diva). Remove infected plants.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Viral diseases — 5 Reference — viral diseases
b: | Virus | Vector | Hosts | Severity | Control | | TMV/ToMV | Mechanical | Tomato, pepper, eggplant | Moderate | Resistant varieties, hygiene | | Curly top | Beet leafhopper | Tomato, bean, beet | Year-dependent | Row cover, resistant varieties | | TSWV | Western flower thrips | Tomato, pepper, lettuce | Moderate | Resistant varieties, thrips control | | Cucumber mosaic | Aphids | Cucurbits, peppers | Moderate | Resistant varieties, aphid control |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders
b: Blossom-end rot · hollow stem · tipburn · buttoning · sunscald · splitting Physiological disorders look like disease but aren't caused by pathogens. They're caused by environmental stress, nutrient imbalance, or cultivar limitations. The Dragoons climate produces a particular set of them — wide diurnal swings, intense sun, dry air, alkaline soil, monsoon rain shock — and several are major causes of crop loss that get misdiagnosed as disease.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders — 1 Blossom-end rot (BER)
b: Dark sunken spot on the blossom end of tomato, pepper, eggplant, squash, watermelon. Cause: calcium not reaching the developing fruit — almost always a delivery problem (irregular watering, root damage, pH imbalance) rather than a supply problem (true calcium deficiency in soil or nutrient solution). Soil control. Consistent watering. Mulch to even out soil moisture. Soil pH 6.0–7.0 (alkaline Cochise water is usually fine for calcium availability). Avoid excessive nitrogen, which dilutes calcium uptake. Hydroponic control. See Guide 4 Part 1. Maintain pH 5.8–6.2, EC below 3.0, and mix calcium nitrate last in nutrient prep. Almost always a dosing or pH problem, not a Ca deficit.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders — 2 Tipburn
b: Brown necrotic margins on inner leaves of lettuce, cabbage, brussels sprouts, and other heading crops. Caused by calcium failure during rapid growth, often combined with heat stress. Same root mechanism as BER — calcium is the most water-mobility-dependent macronutrient. Control. Even watering. Avoid heat stress (shade cloth in late spring); choose tipburn-resistant varieties (modern lettuce hybrids commonly are).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders — 3 Hollow stem
b: Hollow cavities in the main stem and floret stems of broccoli and cauliflower. Caused by boron deficiency, often combined with very rapid growth (high nitrogen + plenty of water + warm soil). Control. Soil test for boron. Side-dress with boron only if test shows deficit — boron toxicity is easy to cause. Slow growth slightly with moderate nitrogen.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders — 4 Buttoning
b: Premature, tiny head formation on brassicas — broccoli or cauliflower that produces a marble-sized head and gives up. Caused by cold stress on young transplants (more than 10 days below 50 °F before establishment). Control. Don't transplant brassicas before the soil is consistently above 60 °F. Don't expose seedlings to early frost.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders — 5 Sunscald
b: White or tan papery patches on the sun-facing side of peppers, tomatoes, apples, melons, even tree trunks. Caused by direct intense sun on tissue not previously shaded. Control. Don't strip foliage above ripening fruit; leave shade on the south and west sides. 30 % shade cloth during the worst June heat. Orient rows north-south for even sun exposure. White latex trunk paint on young fruit trees (50/50 with water).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders — 6 Fruit splitting / cracking
b: Sudden splitting of ripe or near-ripe tomatoes, melons, grapes, pomegranates, figs after a heavy monsoon rain. The fruit took up water faster than the skin could expand. Control. Pick fruit at "color break" before a forecast heavy rain. Mulch heavily to even out soil moisture. Drip irrigation rather than long deep weekly soaks (the latter pattern primes splitting when rain hits). Crack-resistant varieties (Juliet, Mountain Pride, Sungold among tomatoes; Suri Sahel, Wonderful among pomegranates).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Physiological disorders — 7 Other physiological problems
b: | Disorder | Crops | Cause | Control | | Catfacing | Tomato | Cold during flower set | Plant after consistent 55 °F+ nights | | Bolt-before-set | Brassicas, lettuce, cilantro | Heat stress / day length | Plant in proper season; bolt-slow varieties | | Cat-eye / zippering | Tomato | Cold during set | Wait for warmer nights | | Sun-scorched fruit | Peppers, tomato | Direct sun, dehydration | Leave shading foliage | | Tomato leaf roll | Tomato | Heat stress, often harmless | Mulch, irrigation | | Hollow heart | Potato | Inconsistent water | Even watering | Most "disease" problems in the Dragoons are actually physiological. The wide diurnal swings, intense sun, dry air, and monsoon rain shock cause more visible damage than any pathogen except powdery mildew and peach leaf curl. Before reaching for a fungicide or assuming a virus, check water consistency, pH, soil temperature, and recent weather. Most often the answer is "mulch better and water more evenly."

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Small mammals
b: Packrat · gopher · ground squirrel · jackrabbit · cottontail The Dragoons have a richer small-mammal community than most American gardening regions. The four below are the ones that pressure gardens with any regularity.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Small mammals — 1 Packrat (woodrat)
b: Neotoma albigula (white-throated woodrat) is the species you'll meet most often. The packrat is the iconic Cochise County mammal — a stocky brown rodent the size of a small squirrel, with a furry (not naked) tail. Builds large stick-and-debris nests (middens) in cholla, prickly pear, woodpiles, and outbuildings. Identification. Stick middens 2–4 ft across, often with bones, glass, foil, and shiny objects woven in. Chewed irrigation lines. Squash and melon fruit gnawed with tooth marks larger than a mouse but smaller than a rabbit. Tomato fruit half-eaten. Damage in the garden. Chews drip lines (they're attracted to water flow). Eats squash, melon, tomato fruit. Strips bark from young trees in winter for moisture. Nests in outbuildings, sheds, vehicles. Control. Habitat reduction. Move woodpiles, junk piles, dead cholla, and brush away from the garden. The packrat lives where its midden goes — eliminate midden sites near the garden. Snap traps baited with peanut butter, set near runs and at midden entrances. The largest snap trap (rat-sized, not mouse-sized) is what you want. Live traps if you prefer relocation — but they'll come back from anywhere under a mile, so relocation needs to be far. Don't poison. Secondary poisoning kills the snakes, hawks, and owls that handle packrats for free. Cholla and prickly pear within 50 ft of the garden are prime midden habitat — clear them or accept the trade-off. The packrat is opportunistic about water sources. The single fastest way to…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Small mammals — 2 Gopher
b: Pocket gopher (Thomomys bottae) — solitary, tunnel-dwelling rodent. Active year-round in the Dragoons. Lives underground, rarely seen above; identified by characteristic fan-shaped soil mounds. Damage. Eats roots of trees, perennials, root crops. Can kill a young fruit tree in a single season — symptoms are sudden wilt and decline with no above-ground cause, then the trunk wobbles when pushed because the roots are gone. Control sequence. Wire baskets at planting. Quarter-inch galvanized hardware cloth shaped into a 24-inch cube around the root ball when planting any tree or perennial. Standard practice in the Dragoons; absence is a major preventable loss. Active trapping. Macabee or Cinch traps set in fresh mounds when gophers are detected. Two traps in each tunnel, facing opposite directions. Snake habitat. A bull/gopher snake population in the yard is the longest-term control. See §15. Don't bait with strychnine or anticoagulant grain bait in orchard soil — secondary poisoning of hawks, owls, and dogs is a serious risk.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Small mammals — 3 Ground squirrels
b: Round-tailed ground squirrel (Spermophilus tereticaudus) and rock squirrel (Spermophilus variegatus) — both present in the Dragoons. Burrowing diurnal rodents; rock squirrels especially are bold and persistent. Damage. Eat fruit, nuts, melons, sweet corn, sometimes tomatoes. Strip seeds from sunflowers. Tunnel into garden beds. Rock squirrels can climb fruit trees. Control. Trapping with snap traps or live traps; rebait daily. Hardware cloth burial at fence line — 12 inches below grade prevents tunnel entry. Owl boxes. Encourage great horned owl on the property; one owl can eat 1,000+ rodents per year.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Small mammals — 4 Jackrabbit and cottontail
b: Two species, different management: Black-tailed jackrabbit (Lepus californicus) — large (5–8 lb), long ears, leaps 6+ ft. Cuts through young trees and strips bark in winter. Can clear a 4-ft fence. Desert cottontail (Sylvilagus audubonii) — smaller (2–3 lb). Eats lettuce, peas, beans, brassica seedlings, low fruit. Damage. Bark stripping on young trees in winter (jackrabbit). Seedling and low-leaf grazing (both). Sharp 45-degree pruner-cuts on stems are the classic rabbit signature. Control. Welded-wire fence, 5 ft minimum for jackrabbits, 4 ft for cottontails. Bury 6 inches deep or pin tight to ground. Hardware-cloth tree tubes around young trunks, lower 24 inches, in winter. Owl, hawk, coyote populations all eat rabbits — leave habitat for predators.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Large mammals
b: Javelina · deer · mule deer

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Large mammals — 1 Javelina — the Cochise large-mammal threat
b: Pecari tajacu — collared peccary. The Dragoons signature large mammal, and the single most important wildlife threat to fruits, melons, and unfenced gardens. Plant the fence before the trees. A herd that learns where the figs, pomegranates, or melons are will return every year for the rest of those plants' lives. The unfenced backyard orchard in the Dragoons is a javelina restaurant. Exclusion before they discover the food is dramatically easier than removal after. Identification. Pig-like, 30–60 lb adults, gray-black with a pale collar. Travel in herds of 6–15. Mostly nocturnal but visible at dusk and dawn. Strong musky scent at scent posts and bedding areas. Damage. Eats melons, figs, pomegranates, sweet potatoes, prickly pear fruit, almost anything ripe. Pushes over small trees to reach fruit. Strips bark from young fruit trees for moisture. Roots up bulbs and root crops. Tears apart compost piles. Favorite targets. Figs (especially ripe), pomegranates (Wonderful especially), melons of all kinds, sweet potatoes when harvested, peaches that drop to the ground. Exclusion — what works: 5-ft welded-wire fence as a minimum perimeter. T-post every 6 ft, fence buried or pinned at ground level. Javelina dig under more often than they climb. 6-ft fence with one electrified strand at 12 inches above ground — the height of a javelina nose. A 0.5-joule charger and a single hot wire is dramatically more effective than 4 ft of unmanaged wire alone. Trunk wraps. Corrugated drain pipe…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Large mammals — 2 Deer
b: White-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) at higher Dragoon elevations; mule deer (Odocoileus hemionus) at lower. Both browse hard on most garden plants. Damage. Strips foliage from fruit trees, peppers, beans, tomatoes. Stripped bark in winter. Worse in drought years when wild forage is poor. Control. 8-ft fence is the only reliable exclusion. Lower fences (6 ft) work some places but mule deer can clear 6 ft. Two-fence system — two 5-ft fences set 4 ft apart can work where 8-ft is prohibited; deer won't jump into the narrow space. Tree tubes for individual orchard trees in fenced gardens. Motion-activated sprinklers — short-term effect (4–8 weeks before habituation). Liquid Fence and similar repellents — moderate effect, must be reapplied after rain.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Birds
b: Netting · sparrow attacks · dove pressure · the harvest-first strategy Bird pressure in the Dragoons is real but selective. Most birds are not pests; some flocking species hit specific crops hard at specific times.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Birds — 1 The species that matter
b: Curve-billed thrasher (Toxostoma curvirostre) — eats figs, peaches, apricots, tomatoes. Year-round resident. Northern mockingbird (Mimus polyglottos) — same target list as thrasher. Year-round. House finch (Haemorhous mexicanus) — strips young plantings of leaves, eats berries. Year-round. House sparrow (Passer domesticus) — attacks newly seeded beds, strips seedlings. Worst in spring. White-winged dove (Zenaida asiatica) — eats grain, sunflower, sweet corn. Summer migrant. Mourning dove (Zenaida macroura) — same as white-wing. Scrub jay, gray-breasted jay — opportunistic; eat almonds, sometimes fruit. Cedar waxwing (Bombycilla cedrorum) — flocks descend on stone-fruit trees just before harvest.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Birds — 2 Defenses
b: Bird netting — 3/4 inch mesh, draped over fruiting trees or vines just before veraison (first color change). The single most effective tool. Drape, don't tent — drape allows physical contact at canopy edges, where birds learn quickly there's no food. Tent-style structures with airspace let birds get inside and trap them (mortality + crop loss). Drape, then leave. Once netted, don't lift for partial harvests; lift only on harvest morning. Partial coverage teaches the birds where to enter. Harvest-first strategy on figs. Visit the tree daily during ripening. Pick fruit at just-ripe before birds find it. A tree visited daily often loses very little; a tree left alone for three days can lose 30 % in one bad weekend. Closed-eye fig varieties (Celeste) resist bird entry compared to open-eye varieties (Mission, Brown Turkey). Reflective tape, fake owls, mylar balloons — minor effect, birds habituate within 1–2 weeks. Avoid bird mortality. Use small-mesh netting (1/4 to 3/4 inch). Larger mesh tangles wings. Inspect netting daily during use — birds occasionally still get caught and need release.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Birds — 3 Sparrow and dove pressure on bare beds
b: Newly seeded beds — beets, carrots, lettuce — get attacked by sparrows and doves at germination. Common in spring. Control. Floating row cover until plants are 3 inches tall. Bird tape or scare balloons for short-term protection (effective for 1–2 weeks before habituation). Pre-germination indoors. Pre-sprout seeds and transplant — bypasses the seedling-vulnerability window.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Birds — 4 Sweet corn — the dove and crow problem
b: Sweet corn at silking attracts white-winged doves and Chihuahuan ravens. Control. Plant in a block, not a row — denser plantings reduce per-plant access. Bird tape on tassels. Tight-husked varieties (Country Gentleman, Silver Queen) resist bird entry to the ear. Bird netting can kill. Snakes, lizards, and birds themselves all get tangled in poorly maintained or oversized-mesh netting. Use small-mesh netting (1/4 to 3/4 inch). Inspect netting daily. Remove and store netting immediately after harvest — don't leave it up year-round. The wildlife mortality from chronic netting is real and avoidable.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Reptiles as garden allies
b: Gopher snake · kingsnake · whipsnake · why to leave them alone The Dragoons are home to a remarkable reptile diversity, and most of it is on your side. The non-venomous snakes in particular are the longest-running rodent and gopher control in the garden. Learning to identify them and leaving them alone is one of the highest-return habits a Cochise County gardener can build.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 1 The gopher snake — your most useful reptile
b: Sonoran gopher snake (Pituophis catenifer affinis) — also called bull snake. Tan to yellow with dark brown blotches, 3–6 ft long, sometimes longer. Active diurnal and crepuscular. Why you want one in the yard. Eats pocket gophers (the name is literal), packrats, ground squirrels, mice, young rabbits. A single resident gopher snake handles more rodents in a summer than any trap, poison, or terrier ever could. Permanent residents are common in Cochise County yards with adequate shelter. How to recognize. Long, slender. Yellow-tan with squared brown blotches running the length of the back. Pointed head, round pupils, no rattle. When threatened, may flatten its head, vibrate its tail in dry leaves to mimic a rattlesnake, and hiss — completely harmless, designed to scare predators away. The rattlesnake confusion. Gopher snakes often mimic rattlesnake threat display. Look for: (1) head shape — gopher snake has a long pointed head, rattlesnake has a triangular wide head, (2) tail — gopher snake has a tapered pointed tail, rattlesnake has the characteristic segmented rattle, (3) pupils — gopher snakes have round pupils, rattlesnakes have vertical slit pupils. From a safe distance the tail tells you the most. How to encourage. Leave brush piles, woodpile edges, and rock features at the property edge. Provide shelter — a sheet of plywood on the ground in a quiet corner is a popular bull-snake refuge. Don't kill any non-venomous snake in the yard. Don't use rodenticides that…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 2 Kingsnake
b: Sonoran mountain kingsnake (Lampropeltis pyromelana) and California kingsnake (Lampropeltis californiae) — both present in the Dragoons. Kingsnakes eat rodents and other snakes including rattlesnakes. The mountain kingsnake is the brilliant red-black-and-white-banded snake you'll occasionally see in the Dragoons. Why you want one. Kingsnakes are immune to rattlesnake venom and actively hunt them. A resident kingsnake reduces rattlesnake encounter risk around the house. How to recognize. California kingsnake is banded black and white (sometimes black and yellow). Sonoran mountain kingsnake has red-black-white bands — the order matters: "red touch black, friend of Jack; red touch yellow, kill a fellow" distinguishes the harmless mountain kingsnake from the venomous coral snake (which has red touching yellow — but the western coral snake is rare in the Dragoons region).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 3 Other harmless snakes you might see
b: Coachwhip (Coluber flagellum) — fast diurnal, slender, pink-red or tan; eats lizards and small mammals. Western patch-nosed snake (Salvadora hexalepis) — small, fast; eats lizards. Glossy snake (Arizona elegans) — nocturnal, eats rodents. Lyresnake, night snake (Hypsiglena) — small, rear-fanged, mildly venomous but no threat to humans.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 4 The one snake to identify and respect
b: Western diamondback rattlesnake (Crotalus atrox) — present throughout Cochise County. Triangular head, segmented rattle, vertical pupils. Most active April–October. Will rattle as a warning if approached. The encounter protocol. Stop and back away slowly. Give the snake at least 6 ft of space. Don't kill it. Even rattlesnakes serve their ecological role, and killing one tends to bring others (the territory remains, the prey base remains). Call a relocator. In the Sierra Vista / Tombstone / Bisbee area, several local relocators will move a snake for free; in remoter parts of Cochise County, contact Arizona Game and Fish for advice. Train pets. Rattlesnake-aversion training for dogs is widely available in Cochise County and dramatically reduces the bite risk. The most important reptile fact for a Dragoons gardener: the snake in the woodpile is almost certainly a gopher snake, not a rattlesnake. Learn the tail difference — pointed tip versus segmented rattle — and you'll recognize the right snake at 10 ft. A resident gopher snake is the cheapest, longest-lasting rodent control system available, and the kingsnake (rare but present) adds rattlesnake control on top of that.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Reptiles as garden allies — 5 Lizards as allies
b: Desert spiny lizard, side-blotched lizard, whiptail — all eat insects (grasshoppers, beetles, occasionally hornworm larvae). Encourage by leaving sunny rocks and rock-pile habitat. Greater earless lizard — fast, common, harmless. Gila monster (Heloderma suspectum) — venomous but slow and not aggressive. Protected by state law. Leave alone if seen.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Dogs as garden security
b: Breed considerations · training · produce safety A dog on the property is the single most effective wildlife deterrent for some pressure types. Not all dogs and not all situations, but the right dog handles javelina, deer, jackrabbit, and packrat presence by simple deterrence.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Dogs as garden security — 1 What a dog can do
b: Javelina deterrence. A dog actively patrolling — barking, occasionally chasing to the fence — keeps most javelina herds out of the immediate yard. A herd will sometimes test, but rarely settles. Deer deterrence. Less reliable; deer pressure depends heavily on local population density. A dog helps but doesn't replace fencing. Rabbit and ground squirrel deterrence. Most dogs catch or chase rabbits and squirrels; over weeks, the rabbit population learns to feed elsewhere. Packrat deterrence. Some dogs are hunters; others ignore packrats. Terriers and small hunters are best at this.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Dogs as garden security — 2 What a dog can't do
b: Doesn't replace fencing for an orchard or vineyard. The dog isn't watching 24 hours; the fence is. Doesn't help with birds. Most dogs don't engage with birds reliably. Doesn't help with insect pests, obviously. Doesn't help much with snakes — and an untrained dog into a rattlesnake is a serious veterinary emergency.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Dogs as garden security — 3 Breeds suited to the Dragoons
b: The right dog depends on the workload, the size of the property, and the climate (the Dragoons reach 100 °F in June and 18 °F in January). Heat-tolerant working breeds — Australian shepherd (with regular shade), Anatolian shepherd, Karelian bear dog. These are perimeter and livestock-guardian breeds. Heat-tolerant smaller hunters — Jack Russell terrier (packrat / rodent), rat terrier, fox terrier. Smaller, more nimble, good in dense plantings. Avoid heavy-coated breeds without serious shade and water. A Newfoundland is miserable in June Dragoons.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Dogs as garden security — 4 Training for produce safety
b: A garden-trained dog learns to: Not dig in beds. Train with "off" command and consistent correction at the first attempt. A dog that learns to dig in a tomato bed in week one will dig forever. Not eat tomatoes, melons, peppers off the vine. Many dogs naturally avoid them; some develop a habit. Correct early. Patrol the fence line. Walk the perimeter together early — the dog learns the boundary as its territory. Rattlesnake aversion. Worth the one-day class for any Dragoons dog. Local trainers offer this annually around Sierra Vista and Tucson. Some garden plants are toxic to dogs. Tomatoes (foliage, green fruit), onion family (all parts), grapes and raisins, rhubarb leaves, some mushrooms. Most dogs avoid them naturally but supervise new dogs in the garden initially. Keep onion and garlic harvests off the ground where the dog can reach.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fencing & exclusion
b: What works for each animal · cost · siting · sub-grade burial Fencing is the single largest infrastructure investment in pest management. A well-sited 8-ft deer fence around a half-acre orchard costs $2,500–4,000 in materials and 30–60 hours of work; the same orchard fence-free averages 50 %+ wildlife losses per year, indefinitely. The math favors fencing almost always.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fencing & exclusion — 1 Animal-by-animal exclusion requirements
b: | Animal | Minimum fence | Notes | | Cottontail rabbit | 3-ft welded wire, pinned to ground | 1-inch or smaller mesh at bottom | | Jackrabbit | 5-ft welded wire | 1-inch mesh; bury or pin | | Ground squirrel | 4-ft + 12-inch buried apron | Hardware cloth on burial portion | | Pocket gopher | Hardware-cloth basket at planting | Per tree / perennial | | Packrat | Hardware cloth at fence skirt | Move woodpile / cholla habitat | | Javelina | 5-ft welded wire minimum; 6-ft + electric strand at 12" preferred | Bury or pin tight to ground | | Deer | 8-ft fence | Or 2× 5-ft fences set 4 ft apart | | Birds | Bird netting over fruiting tree/vine | Drape, not tent; 3/4-inch mesh |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fencing & exclusion — 2 Material specifications
b: Welded wire (galvanized, 14 or 16 gauge) is the workhorse fencing material. 2x4-inch mesh for most uses; 1-inch mesh at the bottom 18 inches if rabbits or squirrels are in play. Roll widths typically 4 ft, 5 ft, or 6 ft. Hardware cloth (1/4 or 1/2 inch galvanized mesh) for buried portions, tree baskets, gopher exclusion. More expensive but indispensable where it matters. T-posts every 6 ft for typical garden fence; every 8 ft for heavier orchard fence with bigger gauge wire. Drive posts 18 inches into ground. Electric strand — 14-gauge poly wire, 0.5 to 2 joule charger, single strand at 12 inches off ground for javelina. Don't use barbed wire — wildlife mortality is high and the deterrent value isn't better than smooth wire.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fencing & exclusion — 3 Siting considerations
b: Map the runs. Animals travel on predictable paths — wash bottoms, ridge tops, fence lines, dirt road edges. Site your fence to block the run, not at random. Gate placement. Put gates where they're not on the run. A gate on the deer trail is a deer trail with a gate. Don't fence in a hawk corridor. Open shooting lanes for raptors are good for rodent control. A bowed-top deer fence with overhead wire is the worst case. Tree-line edges. Many predators (and some pests) move along treelines. Keep at least a 4 ft mowed strip between woods and garden. Drainage. Don't site fence on a wash centerline. Build gabion or rip-rap if you must cross a wash.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fencing & exclusion — 4 Sub-grade burial
b: For animals that dig under (javelina, ground squirrel, gopher, rabbit): Apron method. Bury 12 inches of hardware cloth at 90-degree angle outward from the fence base. When animal tries to dig, hits the apron and turns back. Skirt method. Pin fence wire tight to ground with landscape staples every 12 inches. Less expensive, less reliable than apron. Trench method. Bury 12–18 inches of fence vertically below grade. Most expensive but most secure.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Fencing & exclusion — 5 Cost ranges (2026 dollars)
b: | Fence type | Linear-foot cost (materials) | Labor (DIY hours / 100 ft) | | 4-ft welded wire, T-posts | 4–6 | 4–6 | | 5-ft welded wire, T-posts | 5–7 | 5–7 | | 6-ft welded wire, T-posts | 7–10 | 6–8 | | 8-ft deer fence, posts | 12–18 | 8–12 | | Hardware cloth (3 ft tall, buried apron) | +5–8 | +3–5 | | Single electric strand | +1–2 | +2 (plus charger 80–200) | | Tree-individual basket (1/2" hardware cloth, 4 ft tall) | 25–40/tree | 1 hr/tree | For a typical 100 ft × 100 ft (400 ft perimeter) garden: Cottontail-only 3-ft fence: ~1,600 in materials. Jackrabbit + javelina 5-ft + electric: ~2,800 in materials. Deer-rated 8-ft fence: ~5,500 in materials. Tree baskets for 20 fruit trees: ~600. The Cochise County fencing pattern that works: a 5-ft welded wire perimeter with a single electric strand at 12 inches (javelina + most rabbits + some deer), individual hardware-cloth tree baskets on every fruit tree (gopher + bark-stripping rabbit), and 3/4-inch bird netting on tree canopies during fruiting only. This sits at roughly half the cost of an 8-ft deer fence and handles 90 % of typical Dragoons wildlife pressure. The exception is heavy deer pressure on the western Dragoons foothills, where the 8-ft fence is genuinely necessary.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them
b: Parasitic wasps · lacewings · ladybugs · syrphid flies · insectary strips The beneficial insect community is the long-term solution to most pest pressure. A garden that has them needs less spraying. A garden that doesn't, never quite gets ahead. This chapter is about how to invite them in and keep them around.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 1 The major beneficials
b: | Beneficial | Targets | What it looks like | How to attract | | Lacewing (Chrysoperla) | Aphids, mites, thrips | Green delicate adults; brown alligator-shaped larvae | Cilantro, dill, fennel flowers; don't spray | | Ladybug (Hippodamia, Harmonia) | Aphids, mealybugs | Red/orange round adults; black/orange alligator larvae | Pollen flowers, overwintering shelter | | Syrphid fly (Toxomerus, Eupeodes) | Aphids | Hovering bee-mimic adults; gray-green larvae | Sweet alyssum, yarrow, dill | | Parasitic wasp (Cotesia, Aphidius, Trichogramma) | Caterpillars, aphids, eggs | Tiny (1–5 mm) wasps; rarely seen | Small-flower forage (cilantro, dill, alyssum) | | Predatory mite (Phytoseiulus, Neoseiulus) | Spider mites | Microscopic | Avoid sulphur/broad-spectrum sprays | | Ground beetle | Cutworms, slugs | Glossy black running beetles | Mulch, stone piles | | Spider | Generalist | Various | Leave webs in non-traffic areas | | Tachinid fly | Caterpillars | Bristly gray flies | Same forage as parasitic wasps | | Assassin bug | Generalist | Long-legged predator bugs | Mixed habitat | | Minute pirate bug | Thrips, mites, aphids | Tiny (2 mm) black-white bug | Small flowers |

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 2 Insectary strips — the canonical Dragoons mix
b: A 12 to 24-inch-wide flowering strip adjacent to the main beds is the single best beneficial-insect habitat. The Dragoons-appropriate mix: | Plant | Role | Season | | Cilantro / coriander | Parasitic wasp forage when in flower | Spring + fall | | Dill | Parasitic wasp + syrphid forage | Spring–summer | | Fennel | Long-season parasitic wasp forage | Late spring–fall | | Yarrow (Achillea) | Lacewing + parasitic wasp forage | Summer | | Sweet alyssum (Lobularia) | Syrphid fly forage (massive) | Cool seasons | | Buckwheat | Generalist forage | Summer | | Borage | Bee forage + parasitic wasp | Summer | | Bachelor's button (Centaurea) | Pollinator + lacewing | Spring | | Native sunflower (Helianthus annuus) | Generalist | Summer–fall | | Black-eyed Susan (Rudbeckia) | Generalist | Summer–fall | The principle: let things flower. Cilantro that bolts is good. Dill gone to seed is good. A row of bolted brassicas in spring is some of the highest-quality parasitic-wasp forage available in the Dragoons. Don't pull the bolt before the wasps find it. The Dragoon native oaks (Quercus emoryi — Emory oak — and Quercus arizonica — Arizona white oak) are exceptional parasitic-wasp habitat. The complex bark, lichen cover, and seasonal small-insect populations support large wasp colonies. If you have native oaks on the property, leave them — they're doing more pest control than any spray you could buy.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 3 The "never spray during bloom" rule
b: Never spray pollinator forage in bloom. Bees, native bees, and the beneficial-insect community are all most exposed when plants are flowering. Even "soft" sprays — soap, neem, spinosad — can harm them. The hard rule: if it's flowering and pollinators are visiting, don't spray. If you must intervene during bloom, hand-pick or water-blast only. This is especially important for: Brassicas in flower (bolted, or seed crops). Spring brassica bloom is critical native-bee forage in the Dragoons. Cucurbits in flower. Squash bees (Peponapis pruinosa) are specialists that emerge with the first squash bloom. Beans in flower. Heavy bumble bee forage. Stone fruit in bloom. Peach bloom is one of the earliest spring forage events. Native plants in bloom near the garden. Mesquite, oak, manzanita, agave — all in the wild beneficial-insect supply chain.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 4 Buying versus attracting
b: Most commercial beneficial-insect releases (ladybug packets, lacewing eggs, predatory mites) have mixed track records in open gardens — the insects often disperse within hours. They work in greenhouses and indoor production where the insects are confined. For outdoor gardens, the better long-term return is on habitat rather than direct release. The native beneficial-insect community is already in the Dragoons — if you stop spraying and start flowering, they show up on their own. The exceptions worth purchasing: Predatory mites for greenhouse / hydroponic systems with spider mite problems. Trichogramma wasp cards for moderate-scale corn earworm or codling moth pressure (timed release). Beneficial nematodes for soil-grub and root maggot problems (genuine effect when properly applied).

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Beneficial insects & how to attract them — 5 What kills beneficials
b: | Practice / spray | Beneficials harmed | | Pyrethrin | Bees, wasps, lacewings, ladybugs, syrphids — almost everything | | Carbaryl (Sevin) | Same | | Imidacloprid (neonicotinoids) | Bees, wasps especially | | Broad-spectrum insecticidal soap | Soft-bodied beneficials (lacewing larvae, syrphid larvae) | | Sulphur | Predatory mites | | Diatomaceous earth | Ground beetles, some lacewing larvae | | Heavy mowing | Ground habitat (ground beetles, spiders) | | Bare-soil weed control | Generalist predator habitat | Most of these can be used selectively without major harm — the rule is targeted, dusk-timed, not during bloom, not on flowering forage.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Spray decision tree
b: When to spray · what to spray · what NOT to spray · dusk timing This chapter is the synthesis of the rest of the guide. Most garden problems do not need a spray. When they do, the choice of spray matters. The framework below works from the gentlest, most targeted tool outward.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Spray decision tree — 1 The decision sequence
b: When a pest or disease is detected, walk this sequence from the top. Stop at the first step that resolves the problem. Most garden issues never get past step 2. Is the population above the action threshold? If not — wait and scout. Most damage is cosmetic and the predators are coming. Is there a cultural fix? Mulch, prune, adjust watering, exclude with row cover, rotate next year. This solves more problems than any spray. Mechanical fix? Hand-pick, hose-blast, sticky trap, pheromone trap. Effective for low-to-moderate populations. Targeted biological? Bt for caterpillars; spinosad for thrips; beneficial nematodes for soil grubs. Species-specific, low impact on the rest of the ecosystem. Targeted soft chemical? Insecticidal soap, horticultural oil, sulphur, copper, sodium bicarbonate. Short residual; spray at dusk. Broad-spectrum (last resort). Pyrethrin, only at dusk, only on non-flowering plants. Acknowledge the collateral damage.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Spray decision tree — 2 The spray inventory — what each one does
b: | Spray | Class | Target | Mode of action | Pollinator safety | Notes | | Insecticidal soap | Soap | Soft-bodied insects (aphid, mite, thrip) | Disrupts cell membranes on contact | Moderate; spray dusk | Must contact insect to work | | Horticultural oil | Oil | Scale, eggs, some mites | Smothers | Moderate | Don't apply >85 °F | | Neem oil | Botanical | Soft-bodied + growth disruption | Disrupts molting, antifeedant | Moderate (some brands worse) | Some neem brands contain phytotoxic carriers | | Bt kurstaki | Biological | Caterpillars only | Gut toxin specific to Lepidoptera | High (safe for bees) | UV-degrades; spray dusk | | Bt israelensis | Biological | Mosquito/fungus-gnat larvae | Gut toxin | High | For aquatic / soil use | | Spinosad | Biological | Caterpillar, thrip, leafhopper, some beetles | Soil-bacterium derivative | Moderate; spray dusk | Bees safe once dried | | Pyrethrin | Botanical | Broad-spectrum insects | Sodium-channel disruption | Low — kills bees on contact | Last resort; dusk only | | Sulphur | Mineral | Mites, powdery mildew | Multiple | Low for predatory mites; bee-safe | Don't apply >85 °F | | Copper (Bordeaux, fixed Cu) | Mineral | Bacterial + fungal | Multiple | Bee-safe but toxic to aquatic life | Dormant-spray peach leaf curl | | Potassium bicarbonate | Mineral | Powdery mildew | Surface pH disruption | Safe | Preventive | | Diatomaceous earth | Mineral | Slugs, soft-bodied insects | Mechanical abrasion | Low (kills beneficials too) | Reapply…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Spray decision tree — 3 The hard rules
b: Three things never to do: Never spray during bloom. Not pyrethrin, not neem, not even spinosad. If a plant is flowering and pollinators are visiting, don't spray it. Never apply copper near waterways. Copper is acutely toxic to fish, frogs, and aquatic invertebrates. Spray dormant orchards far from streams or pond runoff. Don't rinse copper sprayer into a drain that feeds surface water. Never use broad-spectrum sprays during a pest outbreak you don't understand. Pyrethrin makes spider mites worse by killing predatory mites. Carbaryl makes aphids worse by killing aphid predators. Diagnosis before treatment, always.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Spray decision tree — 4 Dusk timing — the safety rule
b: Most beneficial insects (especially bees) stop foraging at sunset. Most pest insects feed at dusk and night. Most sprays that aren't bee-safe become dramatically less harmful applied after sunset. The protocol: spray within 30 minutes of sunset. Allow overnight drying. By morning, most contact-acting sprays (pyrethrin, neem, spinosad) have degraded enough to be relatively safe for pollinators returning. Sprays that should NOT be applied at midday: Pyrethrin (pollinator kill) Neem (pollinator harm + leaf burn at high temperature) Spinosad (pollinator harm before drying) Sulphur (foliage burn above 85 °F) Horticultural oil (foliage burn above 85 °F) Copper (foliage burn at high temperature on some crops) Sprays that can be applied any time: Insecticidal soap (best on dry mornings) Hose-blast water spray (best mornings) Iron phosphate slug bait (apply when ground is moist)

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Spray decision tree — 5 Neem brands — a caveat
b: Not all "neem" is the same. Cold-pressed pure neem oil with no carrier solvents is what the books mean by "neem oil." Cheaper neem products are often clarified neem (hydrophobic extract of neem oil — actually a different chemical profile, less active) or solvent-cut neem in a phytotoxic carrier. Read the label. Pure cold-pressed neem with an emulsifier (and water dilution per label) is what works without burning crops or harming pollinators excessively.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Spray decision tree — 6 The "what to keep in the shed" inventory
b: For a typical Cochise County backyard garden, the practical spray inventory is small: Bt kurstaki (Dipel, Thuricide) — caterpillar control. Replace yearly; loses potency. Insecticidal soap (M-Pede, Safer's, homemade 1 tbsp Castile soap per quart water). Neem oil (pure cold-pressed) — emergency aphid/mite/whitefly control. Spinosad (Monterey Garden Insect Spray) — thrip, codling moth, leafhopper. Sulphur dust or wettable sulphur — powdery mildew + spider mites. Liquid copper (Bonide) — dormant peach leaf curl, fireblight. Potassium bicarbonate (Kaligreen, MilStop) — powdery mildew. Iron phosphate slug bait (Sluggo). Kaolin clay (Surround) — grasshopper, codling moth, sunburn. Diatomaceous earth — cutworm, slug, some beetle. This handful covers nearly every pest and disease in this guide. Replace anything biological (Bt, spinosad, nematodes) annually.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Annual scouting calendar
b: Month-by-month · what to watch for · when controls are needed The Dragoons pest year runs a different rhythm than most American climates — mild winters keep populations alive year-round, the dry pre-monsoon period favors mites, the monsoon brings fungal disease and cucurbit pests, the long fall extends most pest seasons by 4–6 weeks. The calendar below summarizes the 12-month rhythm for the home grower.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Annual scouting calendar — 1 The year in pests
b: | Month | Primary scouting targets | Active controls | | January | Aphids on overwintered brassicas; packrat sign | Dormant copper spray on peach (mid-month); dormant oil on apple/pear | | February | Aphids continue; codling-moth trap setup | Second dormant copper on peach; first compost amendments | | March | Onion thrips on overwintered alliums; flea beetle on greens; cabbage white emerging | Row cover on spring brassicas; trap setup | | April | Cabbage white peak (spring); leafhopper migration begins; spider mites on indoor/protected crops; codling moth flight 1 starts | Bt on brassicas; spinosad if needed; humidity-raise on indoor mites | | May | Cucumber beetle adults emerge; squash vine borer wraps applied; flea beetle on eggplant; spider mites peak (pre-monsoon dry) | Foil collars on squash; row cover on cucurbits; hose-blast for mites | | June | Spider mites peak; codling-moth flight 1 egg-laying; pre-monsoon heat stress (BER, blossom drop) | Mite intervention; codling-moth spinosad; mulch and watering discipline for BER | | July | Monsoon onset — cucumber beetle surge; powdery mildew prevention; tomato hornworm appears; squash vine borer flight | Pyrethrin dusk on cucurbits at first rain; potassium bicarbonate on cucurbits; Bt on hornworm; aluminum foil on squash | | August | Hornworm peak; squash bug peak; cabbage white returning for fall; harlequin bug emerging; powdery mildew everywhere; codling moth flight 2 | Daily dusk scouting (hornworm, harlequin); row cover…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Annual scouting calendar — 2 The four "must-do" dates
b: The four highest-return actions of the Dragoons pest year: Mid-January and mid-February — dormant copper on peach trees. Prevents peach leaf curl. Single most important orchard action. Late May — aluminum foil at base of every squash stem. Prevents squash vine borer. Single most important cucurbit action. First big monsoon rain in July — pyrethrin dusk on cucumber/melon/squash within 48 hours. Controls the cucumber beetle surge that vectors bacterial wilt. Late August — row cover on every fall brassica transplant. Excludes cabbage white, harlequin bug, and cutworm at the most vulnerable stage. Do these four and you handle 60–70 % of typical Dragoons pest losses.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Annual scouting calendar — 3 The dormant-season checklist
b: Winter (December–February) is when most of the next year's pest pressure is decided. The dormant-season checklist: Sanitation. Remove all summer-crop debris. Burn or trash anything that was infected (don't compost). Rake fruit-tree leaves and dispose. Dormant copper sprays. Mid-January and mid-February on peach/nectarine. Single dormant copper on apple/pear if fireblight was an issue. Dormant horticultural oil on apple, pear, and stone fruit. Smothers overwintering scale, mites, and some aphid eggs. Pruning. Cut out fireblight cankers (sterilize between cuts), peach tree borer damage, fruit mummies, broken or crossing limbs. Pheromone trap order. Codling moth, peach tree borer, corn earworm traps for the coming season. Compost and mulch. Build the beds while they're empty. Tool sterilization. Clean and oil pruners; replace blades; sterilize loppers. Plan rotations. No same-family crop in the same bed two years running. Fence inspection. Walk the perimeter. Repair sags, gaps, gate latches. Look for signs of pressure (digging at fence base, browse damage just inside). Soil tests. Order soil tests from a state lab; plan amendments.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Annual scouting calendar — 4 The monsoon-onset trigger
b: The single most important "trigger event" in the Dragoons pest year is the first big monsoon storm — typically late June to mid-July. Within 48–72 hours of that storm: Cucumber beetle populations triple. Bacterial wilt risk peaks. Squash vine borer flight intensifies. Powdery mildew spores germinate. Hornworm moths begin egg-laying. Slug populations explode. Spider mite populations crash (humidity-killed) — moves them off the priority list. Knowing this lets you concentrate action: in the week after the first monsoon storm, plan to scout cucurbits daily, spray potassium bicarbonate preventively on mildew-prone crops, apply pyrethrin dusk to cucurbits if cucumber beetles appear, and scout solanaceae for first hornworm frass. Track the monsoon onset for your specific site. The "first big storm" varies by 2–4 weeks across the Dragoons foothills. The west side (Tombstone, Sierra Vista) usually breaks earlier than the east side (Sunizona, Pearce). Keep a multi-year notebook of monsoon onset dates and your associated pest events — within 3 years you'll have a personal calendar that's more accurate than any regional bulletin.

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Appendix A · Quick-reference table by problem
b: When you find damage and don't know what caused it, this table works backward from symptom to likely cause. | Symptom | Likely cause | Section | | Seedlings cut at soil level overnight | Cutworm | §6.1 | | Wilted vine despite wet soil (cucurbits) | Bacterial wilt (cucumber beetle) | §9.1 | | Wilted plant, gummy frass at base (squash) | Squash vine borer | §5.5 | | Stippled silvery flecks on leaves | Thrips or leafhopper | §4.2, §4.4 | | White powdery coating on leaves | Powdery mildew | §8.1 | | Bronzed stems on tomato | Tomato russet mite | §7.2 | | Holes in brassica leaves | Cabbage white / looper | §5.1 | | Bright orange-and-black bugs on brassica | Harlequin bug | §5.3 | | Pinholes in eggplant or mustard | Flea beetle | §5.8 | | Large green caterpillar on tomato | Hornworm | §5.2 | | Wormy fruit, entry hole at calyx | Codling moth | §5.7 | | Curled puckered red leaves on peach | Peach leaf curl | §8.3 | | Black "shepherd's-crook" tips on apple/pear | Fireblight | §9.3 | | Cupped distorted leaves on tomato | Curly top virus | §10.2 | | Mosaic yellow pattern on cucurbit | Cucumber mosaic | §10.4 | | Dark sunken spot on tomato bottom | Blossom-end rot | §11.1 | | Brown necrotic margin on lettuce inner leaves | Tipburn | §11.2 | | Hollow stem in broccoli | Boron deficiency | §11.3 | | Tiny broccoli head ("buttoning") | Cold stress on young transplant | §11.4 | | Cracking tomato fruit after rain | Splitting | §11.6 | | Sunken white papery patch on fruit | Sunscald | §11.5 | |…

sh: Appendix B · Further reading and resources
b: University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Cochise County office. The canonical source for region-specific pest, disease, and crop alerts. Bulletins, master gardener program, soil and plant diagnostic services. Arizona Department of Agriculture. Pesticide regulation, licensed-applicator information, regulated-species permits (javelina, deer). Arizona Game and Fish. Wildlife relocation, conflict-resolution advice, hunting and permit information. Books worth owning: Garden Insects of North America — Whitney Cranshaw. The standard pest identification reference. The Holistic Orchard — Michael Phillips. The deepest practical orchard IPM reference. The Family Garden Journal — Native Seeds/SEARCH (Tucson). Sonoran Desert-region grower's calendar. Insects of the Texas Lost Pines — Stephen Welton Taber. Despite the title, broadly applicable to Southwest insect identification. A Field Guide to Western Reptiles and Amphibians — Robert Stebbins. Snake and lizard identification. Online resources: University of Arizona College of Agriculture publications — cals.arizona.edu — extensive Arizona-specific bulletins. Native Seeds/SEARCH — nativeseeds.org — heritage Southwest varieties, many of them disease/pest-resistant. Bugguide.net — the standard insect identification community resource. iNaturalist.org — submit photos for community ID of unfamiliar pests, plants, and wildlife. Local relationships worth building: The Cochise County Cooperative Extension office in Sierra Vista. Free soil…

gt: Pest, Disease & Wildlife in the Dragoons
sh: Closing notes
b: The Dragoons garden is unusual among American gardens. The pest community is smaller than most regions, but the species are different enough that a generic gardening book gets it wrong in costly ways. The single biggest mental shift for a newcomer is moving from a "spray-when-you-see-it" reflex to a prevention-first, scout-often, intervene-precisely IPM rhythm. Five habits cover most of what matters: The dusk walk. Ten minutes a day in season. No tool but a flashlight and a jar. The four "must-do" dates. Dormant copper in January and February. Foil collars in May. Pyrethrin within 48 hours of the first monsoon storm. Row cover on fall brassicas in August. The native habitat. Don't tidy the property to lawn-and-bed neatness. Native oak, prickly pear, mesquite, and brush all support the predator community that handles most pest pressure for free. The fence. Plant it before the trees. A javelina-rated, jackrabbit-rated 5-ft fence with a single electric strand is the canonical Cochise County perimeter. The notebook. Year-over-year tracking of pest events on your specific site is more accurate than any regional bulletin. Three years of notes, and you have a personal calendar. The Dragoons garden rewards attention. Most of the work is paying attention before the work is needed. — Guide 10, Dragoon Mountains Growing Guides

sh: How to use this guide
b: Two questions sit upstream of every crop guide in this library: what is the soil under the plant doing, and where did the plant come from. This is the reference where those two questions are answered in depth. Every other guide assumes you know what is in here. The guide is in two parts: Part 1 — Soil-building for the Dragoons (chapters 1–11). The Dragoons baseline, how to read soil, the amendments that move it where you want it, the four composting methods that work in dry-air country, manure sourcing, cover crops, mulching, and a five-year soil-building plan. Part 2 — Seed starting & propagation (chapters 12–24). Seed sourcing, the indoor seed-start setup, germination temperatures by crop, direct-sow vs transplant logic, hardening off, asexual propagation (slips, cuttings, divisions, air layering), saving your own seed, and a month-by-month seed-start calendar for the Dragoons. The two halves connect at the bed. A transplant is only as good as the soil it goes into; a perfectly amended bed wastes the season if you sowed a 90-day variety on June 1. Both halves have to land for a Dragoons garden to perform. If you're new here, read three things first. §1.1 — the Dragoons soil baseline (alkaline, low organic matter, caliche close to the surface) explains why you can't just follow a Pacific-Northwest gardening book. §16 — the germination temperature table tells you when to start every common crop. §24 — the month-by-month seed-start calendar tells you what to start this week…

sh: Part 1 — Soil-building for the Dragoons
Why soil-building is the biggest lever
b: The Dragoons baseline · what compounds over years · the OM-percentage target Most of what separates a productive Dragoons garden from a struggling one is not variety choice, not irrigation gear, not pest pressure — it is the soil. Hydroponics is the explicit workaround (Guides 2 and 3); for everything growing in the ground, soil is the system. And the Dragoons soil baseline is honest about what it asks of you: alkaline, low in organic matter, well-supplied with mineral calcium and magnesium from the same caliche that gets in your way, and shallow enough in many sites that the caliche pan itself defines the root zone.

sh: Why soil-building is the biggest lever — 1 The Dragoons soil baseline
b: These numbers describe a typical unworked Dragoons bed at 4,500–5,000 ft elevation in the foothills of the range. Site-to-site variation is real — a wash-bottom site can read pH 7.4 and 3% OM, an exposed ridge can read 8.3 and 0.6% — but this is the central tendency. | Parameter | Dragoons typical | What target soils look like | Implication | | pH | 7.6 – 8.1 | 6.0 – 7.0 (most vegetables) | Most micronutrients are partially locked out at this pH; acidification is the highest-leverage chemistry change | | Electrical conductivity (EC) | 0.4 – 1.2 mS/cm | 0.3 – 0.8 mS/cm | Variable — irrigation history and manure history dominate | | Organic matter (OM) | 1 – 2% | 4 – 6% (productive vegetable soil) | The single biggest deficit; nearly every amendment in chapter 3 is aimed at this number | | Texture | Sandy loam to loamy sand; gravel in many sites | Loam to silt loam | Drains too fast; needs OM to hold water | | CEC (cation exchange capacity) | 8 – 15 meq/100 g | 15 – 25 meq/100 g (vegetable soil) | Low — fertilizer leaches fast; small frequent feedings beat big single ones | | Calcium | High (3,000 – 6,000 ppm) | 1,500 – 3,000 ppm | Abundant from caliche; you almost never need to add calcium | | Magnesium | Adequate to high | 300 – 600 ppm | Usually fine; Mg deficiency in the Dragoons is almost always a pH-lockout symptom, not a real deficit | | Phosphorus | Variable — often locked by pH | 30 – 50 ppm available | Even when total P is fine, available P is low at pH > 7.8 | |…

sh: Why soil-building is the biggest lever — 2 Why annual investment compounds
b: A first-year Dragoons bed at 1.5% OM is producing food, but it is producing food the hard way — fast irrigation cycles, frequent fertigation, narrower margins on every crop. Each season's compost top-dress and cover-crop turn-under adds organic matter. The math is slow but real: A 2-inch annual top-dress of finished compost into a 10-inch root zone, fully incorporated, raises OM by roughly 0.5–0.8 percentage points per year — and that number drops over time because microbial respiration burns OM continuously in warm dry soil. A turned-under cover crop adds another 0.2–0.4 percentage points per year, depending on biomass. Counter-eroding all of that is mineralization — the warm dry Dragoons climate burns OM faster than a cooler or wetter region would. The equilibrium ceiling for unirrigated desert soil sits around 1–2% OM; an irrigated, mulched, annually amended Dragoons bed can stabilize at 4–6% OM if you keep at it. Year 1 to year 5 is where the gains are most visible. Bed water-holding doubles. Irrigation frequency drops. Color and texture change — the soil becomes darker, crumbles in your hand, smells like a forest floor instead of like dust. The full multi-year plan is in chapter 11. Take one photo of your bed surface every September, from the same angle, with a quarter (or a hand) for scale. Five years of photos tells the soil-building story better than any data table — and the visible difference between year 1 and year 5 is what keeps the routine going through the…

sh: Reading your soil
b: pH · EC · OM · texture (the jar test) · the UA Cooperative Extension panel Before you amend anything, know what you have. A Dragoons garden ruined by random sulfur additions is just as broken as one ruined by neglect — overshooting pH or salinity is harder to fix than starting alkaline.

sh: Reading your soil — 1 The four numbers that matter
b: These are the four readings worth knowing for any soil bed in the Dragoons. Everything else (boron, sulfate, sodium, individual micros) is useful detail; these four set the strategy. | Reading | What it tells you | How to measure | Frequency | | pH | Whether nutrients are chemically available to roots | Inexpensive probe ($30) or send-out lab | Annual, plus any time growth looks "stuck" | | EC (electrical conductivity) | How salty the soil is (fertilizer + native salts) | Same probe (many do both); or lab | Annual, more often if you use manure | | OM (organic matter, %) | How much carbon is in the soil — proxy for water retention, biology, CEC | Send-out lab (loss-on-ignition); estimate visually | Every 2–3 years | | Texture | Sand / silt / clay ratio — drives water and nutrient behavior | Jar test (free, §2.4) or lab | Once per site; doesn't change |

sh: Reading your soil — 2 pH — the master variable
b: pH (the chemistry term for acidity / alkalinity, on a 0–14 scale where 7 is neutral) determines whether the nutrients in your soil are available to plant roots. The Dragoons soil is alkaline (pH 7.6–8.1) because of three converging conditions: arid climate (rainfall does not leach calcium carbonate out of the profile), parent material rich in limestone and granite, and caliche close to the surface. At pH 7.8 and above: Iron, zinc, manganese, copper become partially insoluble — visible as interveinal chlorosis (yellow leaves with green veins) on the young growth of broccoli, peppers, blueberries, blackberries, and any acid-loving plant. Phosphorus binds with calcium to form insoluble calcium phosphates — the soil test reads "adequate" but the plant cannot reach it. Boron, molybdenum, and sulfate behavior changes; boron uptake declines. Vegetables and most fruit trees prefer pH 6.0–7.0. Brassicas tolerate up to 7.5. Sweet potatoes prefer 5.5–6.5 (Guide 7). Grapes accept 6.0–7.0 (Guide 5). Blueberries demand 4.5–5.5 and are not realistic in unamended Dragoons soil at all.

sh: Reading your soil — 3 EC — soluble salts
b: EC (electrical conductivity, in mS/cm or dS/m — the same unit) is a proxy for total dissolved salts in the soil solution. The Dragoons inherit a moderate native salt load from the parent material; irrigation water (chapter discussion in Guide 13) adds more. The dangerous combination is feedlot manure (often 4–8 mS/cm before aging) added to already-salty soil watered with mineral-rich well water — that stack produces salt-burn on transplants and a salt rind on the bed surface after warm dry weeks. A workable target: bulk soil EC below 1.5 mS/cm (1:1 soil-water slurry method, which is what most home probes use). Above 2.0 mS/cm, you start losing salt-sensitive crops (carrots, onions, lettuce, beans). Above 3.0, almost nothing performs.

sh: Reading your soil — 4 The jar test — measuring soil texture
b: Soil texture (the ratio of sand, silt, and clay) does not change over your lifetime. You only have to measure it once. The jar test is free and reliable. Dig a sample to 8-inch depth. Discard the top half-inch (mostly mulch and organic debris). Crumble out roots and rocks. Aim for 1 cup of soil. Fill a clear quart jar half full with the sample. Add water to about 80% full. Add a teaspoon of dish soap (this breaks up clumps). Cap and shake hard for 1 minute. Set the jar on a level surface and wait. Sand settles in 1 minute. Silt settles in 2 hours. Clay can take 24 hours or more. Read the layers. Mark each band with a marker as it settles. Measure each band's thickness. Sand : silt : clay percentages by depth tell you the texture. Classify using the USDA soil texture triangle. Most Dragoons sites land in sandy loam, loamy sand, or sandy clay loam. A typical Dragoons reading: 65% sand, 20% silt, 15% clay — sandy loam. That texture drains quickly, holds little water, and holds little of any nutrient that isn't tied to organic matter. The fix is not changing texture (impractical) — it is adding organic matter (chapter 3).

sh: Reading your soil — 5 The UA Cooperative Extension soil panel
b: The University of Arizona Cooperative Extension Cochise County office (520-458-8278, Sierra Vista) brokers soil samples through the University of Arizona soil-testing lab in Tucson. The standard agricultural panel runs about $50 and covers: pH, EC, lime requirement Organic matter (%) Available P, K, S, Ca, Mg, Na Available Fe, Zn, Mn, Cu, B Texture (estimated) and cation exchange capacity Recommendations for amendment How to submit: Sample correctly. 8 to 12 sub-samples from across the bed, mixed in a clean bucket, then 1 cup of the mixed composite submitted. Sample 6–8 inches deep for vegetable beds; 12 inches for orchard sites. Air-dry the sample. Spread on newspaper for 2–3 days. Do not oven-dry — it skews several readings. Bag and label with site, date, depth, intended crop. Drop off at the Sierra Vista office or follow current submission instructions on the Cochise County Cooperative Extension web page. Results return in 2–4 weeks. Do the full panel once when you start a new bed, and then a cheaper pH/EC/OM-only panel every other year after that. The micronutrient readings do not move fast in irrigated soil; the macros and OM are the ones that change as you amend. Save the first full panel as your "year zero" baseline.

sh: Reading your soil — 6 Home probes — what they're good for
b: A $30 dual-probe soil meter (pH + moisture) is approximate but useful for trend rather than absolute number. Probes drift, and the calibration on cheap meters is rough. A reading of 7.6 from a probe might be 7.5 or 7.9; what matters is whether next month's reading is higher or lower than this month's. For absolute numbers, defer to the lab. For EC, a hand-held conductivity meter (the same kind used for hydroponics — see Guide 4) works for a 1:1 soil-water slurry reading. Mix one part soil to one part distilled water by volume, stir, settle 30 minutes, decant, measure. Do not use tap water for any home soil-test reading. Cochise County tap water carries 0.6–1.0 mS/cm of its own dissolved minerals, which will swamp any soil EC reading. Use distilled water (gallon jugs at any grocery store) or rainwater for all home tests.

sh: Soil amendments for the Dragoons
b: Compost · aged manure · elemental sulfur · gypsum · biochar This chapter is the amendment reference. Each amendment has a job. None of them are "fertilizer" in the immediate sense — they change the soil's chemistry, structure, or biology over weeks to seasons, and the change is durable.

sh: Soil amendments for the Dragoons — 1 The amendment cheat sheet
b: | Amendment | What it does | Application rate | Cost (Cochise County, 2026) | Dragoons fit | | Finished compost | Adds OM, biology, slow-release N/P/K; buffers pH | 2–3 inches/year, worked into top 8" | 40–70 per cubic yard delivered | ●●●●● — universal foundation | | Aged horse manure | Adds OM, moderate N, K; some salts | 1–2 inches/year, aged 6+ months first | Free to 20/load (chapter 8) | ●●●●○ — workhorse if sourced clean | | Aged cattle manure | Adds OM, slow N; higher salt | 1 inch/year, aged 9+ months | 5–15/cu ft bagged; free farm-direct | ●●●○○ — watch the EC | | Composted chicken manure | High N, P; very hot | ½ inch/year max, fully composted | 10–15 per 40-lb bag | ●●●●○ — small amounts only | | Elemental sulfur | Acidifies soil over months; lowers pH | 1–2 lb / 100 sq ft per season | 15 per 25-lb bag | ●●●●● — the pH lever | | Gypsum (calcium sulfate) | Adds Ca and S without raising pH; flocculates clay | 2–5 lb / 100 sq ft | 10 per 40-lb bag | ●●●○○ — useful in salty or sodium-affected soil only | | Biochar | Adds permanent C, CEC, micropore habitat for biology | 5–10% by volume of root zone, one-time | 20–40 per cu ft (or DIY) | ●●●●○ — long-term play | | Alfalfa meal | N, K, triacontanol (growth stimulant) | 5 lb / 100 sq ft pre-plant | 20 per 40-lb bag (Willcox feed) | ●●●●○ — local, balanced | | Feather meal | Slow-release N (12-0-0) | 2 lb / 100 sq ft pre-plant | 25 per 25-lb bag | ●●●○○ — clean N, slow | | Bone meal | Slow P, Ca | 2 lb / 100 sq ft at…

sh: Soil amendments for the Dragoons — 2 Compost — the foundation amendment
b: Compost does more for a Dragoons bed than any other single amendment. It adds organic matter (the OM number from chapter 2), supplies slow-release N/P/K, increases water-holding capacity, builds CEC, buffers pH downward over time, and brings biology — bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes — that the bed otherwise lacks. Application rate: 2–3 inches of finished compost worked into the top 8 inches of bed, once a year, after the bed is cleared in fall (for spring beds) or in late summer (for fall brassica beds, per Guide 6 §2.1). Heavy first-year application: 4–6 inches when breaking new ground.
